catalog.summary: The "Awesome Claude Skills" list (maintained by BehiSec, contactable via X @Behi_Sec, sponsored by Atlas Cloud) is a community-curated link catalog of Claude skills, skill collections, and related tooling. It contains roughly 170 entries organized into 13 sections: Document Skills (6), Development & Code Tools (39), Data & Analysis (19), Scientific & Research Tools (6), Writing & Research (10), Learning & Knowledge (5), Media & Content (16), Health & Life Sciences (3), Collaboration & Project Management (18), Security & Web Testing (11), Utility & Automation (16), Articles & Blog Posts (1), and Collections (20). It looks current and actively maintained: the most recent visible commit is 2026-06-03 (about six weeks before today), and entries reference 2026-era content (an arXiv 2602 paper on 40,000+ skills, OWASP Top 10:2025 / ASVS 5.0 / Agentic AI 2026 guidance). Quality is mixed by design — it spans official Anthropic and vendor-maintained repos (Anthropic, HashiCorp, Trail of Bits, Qdrant, coinpaprika) alongside many small personal projects, crypto/promotional entries, and mega-collections of varying rigor. Several entries point into shared multi-skill repos (anthropics/skills, sanjay3290/ai-skills, ComposioHQ/awesome-claude-skills, obra/superpowers, michalparkola/tapestry-skills-for-claude-code, mhattingpete/claude-skills-marketplace).

superpowers_overview.overall: Superpowers (v6.1.1) is a mature, battle-tested, opinionated software-development methodology packaged as 14 auto-triggering skills plus a session-start hook that injects the "you must use skills" bootstrap into every session across ten harnesses. Its philosophy is discipline enforcement rather than reference material: skills are treated as behavior-shaping code, authored via a TDD loop (baseline pressure-test agents WITHOUT the skill, document their verbatim rationalizations, write counters, re-test), which is why the flagship skills are full of Iron Laws, rationalization tables, and red-flag lists rather than explanations. The standout strengths are subagent-driven-development (dense operational knowledge about model-tier selection, file-based context handoffs, compaction-surviving progress ledgers, and reviewer-integrity rules that clearly encode expensive real-session failures) and writing-skills (arguably the best public document on authoring agent skills, including the empirically-tested finding that workflow-summarizing descriptions cause agents to skip skill bodies). Weaknesses: the bootstrap's "1% chance = MUST invoke" rule and brainstorming's hard gate on ALL creative work add real ceremony to trivial tasks; a few skills are thin wrappers (executing-plans, requesting-code-review); some motivational statistics are unverifiable; and the system is deeply CLI-harness-centric — the workflow skills assume subagents, bash, and git, so little of the pipeline transfers to claude.ai, though the behavioral skills (receiving-code-review, verification-before-completion, systematic-debugging's framework) port well as standalone content.
superpowers_overview.components: NON-SKILL MACHINERY: (1) hooks/hooks.json — registers a synchronous SessionStart hook (matcher "startup|clear|compact") that runs "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/hooks/run-hook.cmd session-start"; re-fires after /clear and compaction so the bootstrap survives context loss. (2) hooks/session-start — bash script that cat's skills/using-superpowers/SKILL.md, JSON-escapes it via fast bash parameter substitution, wraps it in an <EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>"You have superpowers"</EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT> block, and emits it as hook context in the platform-correct JSON shape (Cursor: snake_case additional_context; Claude Code: hookSpecificOutput.additionalContext; Copilot CLI/others: top-level additionalContext — emitting only one to avoid Claude Code's double-read). This IS the bootstrap: every session starts with the skill-invocation mandate in context, which then drives auto-triggering of all other skills. (3) hooks/run-hook.cmd — cmd.exe/bash polyglot wrapper so the same hook runs on Windows (finds Git Bash in standard paths, silently no-ops if absent) and Unix; hook scripts are extensionless to dodge Claude Code's Windows ".sh → prepend bash" auto-detection. (4) .claude-plugin/plugin.json — plugin manifest (name "superpowers", v6.1.1, MIT, Jesse Vincent); .claude-plugin/marketplace.json — a "superpowers-dev" marketplace pointing at ./ for local dev installs. (5) NO commands/ and NO agents/ directories — zero slash commands; the whole plugin is 14 skills + 1 hook, by design (skills auto-trigger, users type nothing). (6) Multi-harness packaging: parallel manifests for Codex (.codex-plugin), Cursor (.cursor-plugin), Kimi (.kimi-plugin), OpenCode (.opencode/plugins/superpowers.js), Pi (.pi/extensions/superpowers.ts, package.json doubles as Pi manifest), Gemini (gemini-extension.json), Factory Droid (.agents/plugins/marketplace.json), plus hooks-cursor.json — each re-implements the session-start injection for its harness. (7) Skill-embedded executables: subagent-driven-development/scripts/{task-brief, review-package, sdd-workspace} (extract per-task briefs and diff packages to files to keep the orchestrator's context lean); brainstorming/scripts — a zero-dependency ~723-line Node server (server.cjs) plus start/stop shell scripts for the optional "visual companion" browser UI, which phones home a version-tagged logo load to primeradiant.com unless SUPERPOWERS_DISABLE_TELEMETRY/DISABLE_TELEMETRY is set. (8) tests/ — per-harness infrastructure tests (claude-code, codex, cursor-analog kimi, opencode, pi, hooks, shell-lint, brainstorm-server); scripts/ — bump-version.sh, lint-shell.sh, codex packaging/sync; .version-bump.json lists every manifest to keep in version lockstep. (9) CLAUDE.md (symlinked as AGENTS.md) — an unusually aggressive contributor gate aimed at AI agents: cites a 94% PR rejection rate, mandates model/harness disclosure on every PR, bans dependency-adding PRs, "compliance-with-Anthropic-docs" skill rewording without eval evidence, and requires a transcript proving "Let's make a react todo list" auto-triggers brainstorming for any new harness integration. docs/superpowers/{plans,specs}/ show the project dogfooding its own brainstorm→spec→plan workflow.

fable_overview.overall: The repo's philosophy is stated crisply and actually followed: tell the model what to do in what order with numeric thresholds rather than what to value, let every rule earn its place by fixing a measured failure (the v1→v2→v3 INTENT-gate story is the flagship: rule absent = 0/4 Haiku surfacing a spec-vs-test conflict, rule as mid-list prose = 1/4, rule as forced report artifact = 4/4), and ship the eval alongside the skills. Maturity is high for a week-old project (v1.0.0 on 2026-07-06 through v1.2.1 by 2026-07-09): CI consistency checks, plugin+marketplace packaging, 8 fixtures with committed raw judge outputs, and a changelog — but it is days old, single-author, and one artifact (AGENTS.md) has already drifted from its source of truth. The eval harness claims to measure whether a mid-tier model following the loop beats the same model free-styling, specifically at "traps" where the plausible action is wrong; grading is by LLM judges that are ground-truth-anchored and partially objective (they diff run directories against pristine fixtures and re-run code, so "did it edit files" and "do tests pass" are facts, not opinions), but report-quality scoring is subjective, the judge is built on the same frontier model family that appears as a baseline (a self-grading conflict RESULTS.md itself admits), sample sizes are 1-4 runs per cell (the "159 agent runs" headline is plausible only if judges and meta-judges are counted), and models are named only as tier labels (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus/"Fable") with no pinned model IDs — so results are directionally credible smoke tests, and to its great credit the repo says exactly that, reporting four null rounds, a fixture-design failure (9a), and a known open defect (Haiku scaffolding leakage) alongside the wins. Standout strengths: the forced-artifact design insight ("weak models follow rules at decision points, not rules in lists"), the per-domain fraud tables with binding minimum evidence sets, the numeric hard bounds, and the shipped trap fixtures, which are reusable test material for ANY governance library including yours. Standout weaknesses: the repo's own data shows near-zero lift for Sonnet-class-and-up models on small attended tasks (rounds 1, 6, 7 nulls), meaning much of the method is ceremony for a strong model, and fable-loop's orchestration value is entirely unproven. Overlap with your library is real but the framings differ: plan-gate/adversarial-verify/scope-fence are trigger-scoped governors, while fable-method is one monolithic always-on loop — the distinctive content worth harvesting verbatim is the INTENT line + authority order (user > spec > tests > code, "make the tests pass is not a statement of intended behavior"), the quantified triviality gate, the 3-cycle/2-lookup hard bounds, the failed-edit recovery ladder, "a changed test is guilty until its justification traces to a spec", the VERIFIED/CAVEATS/REFUTED verdict triad, and the domain fraud tables.
fable_overview.components: .claude-plugin/plugin.json + marketplace.json: the repo is packaged as a Claude Code plugin named "fable" (v1.2.1) AND its own self-hosted marketplace, so `/plugin marketplace add Sahir619/fable-method` + `/plugin install fable@fable-method` installs all three skills namespaced (/fable:fable-method etc.). install.sh / install.ps1: trivial standalone installers that cp the three skill dirs into ~/.claude/skills/. AGENTS.md: a harness-portable copy of the fable-method body for Codex/Cursor/aider/raw prompts — NOTE it has drifted behind the shipped SKILL.md (missing the v1.2.0 "orient first" rule, the chained-reads parallelization nuance, and the Step 6 scratch-cleanup rule) despite the README calling it "identical". .github/checks.py + workflows/checks.yml: CI consistency checker run on every push/PR — validates manifests parse and versions agree, skill frontmatter, domain-adapter section completeness, evidence JSON well-formedness, scenario integrity (GROUND-TRUTH.md present, never leaked into fixtures), and a house style rule banning em/en dashes. eval/: the machinery behind the claims — workflow.js (a Claude Code Workflow script implementing the A/B harness: Haiku executors run fresh fixture copies under control vs method conditions with an "user is offline" protocol; ground-truth-anchored judges score 0-2 on four criteria and diff run dirs against pristine copies), scenarios/ (8 trap fixtures s1-s8, each with a GROUND-TRUTH.md answer sheet, including the s7 "fraudulent work" crime scene with 5 planted frauds and s8 fraudulent marketing copy with 6), results/ (11 raw sanitized judge-output JSONs with per-run scores, files_changed evidence, and verdict summaries — I spot-checked round 3 and it contains genuine per-run detail), cases/ (one narrative case study per scenario). No hooks and no slash-command files; the skills are the only executable surface. CONTRIBUTING.md sets a "prime directive": no rule ships without a failing trap scenario first, and null results must be logged.

verdicts.name: brainstorming

inv.name: brainstorming
inv.description: You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.
inv.purpose: Forces a Socratic design phase before any code is written: explore project context, ask one question at a time, propose 2-3 approaches, present the design in sections for incremental approval, then write and commit a spec. It is the mandatory entry point of the whole Superpowers pipeline and terminates only by invoking writing-plans.
inv.key_content: A <HARD-GATE> tag forbidding any implementation action until a design is presented and approved, plus an explicit anti-pattern section rejecting 'too simple to need a design' (todo lists and config changes included). 9-item mandatory checklist (each item becomes a todo). Graphviz process flowchart. One-question-per-message rule, multiple-choice preferred. Scope check that flags multi-subsystem requests for decomposition BEFORE refining details. Spec saved to docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md and committed; 4-point spec self-review (placeholder scan, internal consistency, scope, ambiguity) followed by an explicit user review gate with scripted wording. 'Design for isolation and clarity' unit-boundary heuristics. A just-in-time 'visual companion' protocol: never offer upfront, only when a question is genuinely visual, offer must be its own message, and per-question browser-vs-terminal decisions even after acceptance.
inv.surfaces: Both for the dialogue/design portion; full workflow leans Claude Code (commits spec to git, chains into writing-plans, and the optional visual companion runs a local Node server).
inv.dependencies: Chains to superpowers:writing-plans (required next step). Optional: visual-companion.md guide + scripts/ (zero-dep Node server.cjs, start-server.sh/stop-server.sh, frame-template.html, helper.js — requires Node and a browser); spec-document-reviewer-prompt.md; git for committing the spec; the session-start hook to trigger it at all.
inv.quality: Substantive. The hard gate, decomposition-before-questions ordering, spec self-review checklist, and the carefully engineered just-in-time companion offer are all beyond default model behavior. Cost: the no-exceptions gate imposes ceremony on genuinely trivial changes. The visual companion adds significant machinery (and telemetry) for an optional feature. No staleness.

verdicts.overlaps_with: plan-gate (core, /home/user/claude-core-skills/.claude/skills/plan-gate/SKILL.md); Claude Code plan mode (built-in); adversarial-verify (spec self-review portion); conflicts with architecture-contract's anti-ceremony invariant
verdicts.surfaces: Dialogue portion would run on both surfaces, but the full workflow (git-committed spec, mandatory chain into superpowers:writing-plans, Node-based visual companion) is Claude Code-only
verdicts.license: MIT (Copyright 2025 Jesse Vincent)
verdicts.rationale: The core capability — a hard gate forcing goal clarification, 2-3 ranked alternatives, and pre-committed design approval before any implementation — is exactly what plan-gate already provides, and Claude Code plan mode natively covers the interactive present-then-approve loop on that surface; the spec self-review checklist is a weaker subset of adversarial-verify. The genuinely distinctive residue (one-question-at-a-time Socratic elicitation, multiple-choice preference, section-by-section approval) is interaction-style prose a base model performs when asked, failing the same bar that retired live-state-truth. Worse, it is doctrinally incompatible: its no-exceptions HARD-GATE ('a todo list, a config change — all of them' need a presented design) directly contradicts plan-gate's triage rule, which declares ceremony on trivia a hard FAIL, and violates architecture-contract's anti-ceremony invariant — installing both would put two contradictory always-firing gates in every session. It is also pipeline-coupled (terminal state MUST invoke superpowers:writing-plans, superpowers-specific spec path, Node/browser visual companion machinery), so adoption would require substantial rewriting just to coexist. Nothing here measurably raises the ceiling beyond plan-gate + plan mode; skip it.

verdicts.name: dispatching-parallel-agents

inv.name: dispatching-parallel-agents
inv.description: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies
inv.purpose: Teaches when and how to fan out multiple concurrent subagents for independent problems (e.g., unrelated test-file failures), with each agent given a precisely crafted, self-contained prompt rather than inherited session context. Emphasizes preserving the orchestrator's context for coordination.
inv.key_content: Decision flowchart for parallel vs sequential vs single-agent. The concrete mechanic that multiple Task dispatches in ONE response run in parallel while one-per-response runs sequentially. 4-part agent prompt contract (specific scope, clear goal, constraints, expected output) with a full worked example prompt including 'Do NOT just increase timeouts - find the real issue'. Mistake table (too broad / no context / no constraints / vague output). Post-return verification steps: check for conflicts between agents' edits, run full suite, spot-check for systematic errors. When-NOT list: related failures, exploratory debugging, shared state.
inv.surfaces: Claude Code only — requires the Task/subagent tool and parallel dispatch semantics; meaningless on claude.ai.
inv.dependencies: Subagent dispatch capability (general-purpose agents). No scripts or hooks. Complements subagent-driven-development (which explicitly forbids parallel IMPLEMENTATION dispatch — this skill is for independent investigations/fixes).
inv.quality: Moderately substantive. The one-response-equals-parallel mechanic and the prompt-contract template are concrete and useful; the rest (partition independent work, verify integration) is close to what a strong model does unprompted. The 'Real-World Impact' section citing a 2025-10-03 session is narrative padding the project's own writing-skills skill warns against.

verdicts.overlaps_with: Claude Code built-ins (Agent/subagent Task tool docs, Workflow orchestration, and the harness system prompt's own "make independent tool calls in the same block" parallel-dispatch instruction); base-model judgment on partitioning independent work; adversarial-verify (candidate's post-return Verification section is a thin subset of it)
verdicts.surfaces: Claude Code only — requires the Task/subagent dispatch tool; inert on claude.ai, one of the owner's two equally weighted surfaces

Its 4-part agent-prompt contract (scope, goal, constraints, expected output) restates what the Task tool documentation already requires, because subagents get no inherited context by construction. Its post-return verification steps (check for conflicting edits, run full suite, spot-check) are a shallow subset of the core adversarial-verify skill, and its when-to-parallelize flowchart is judgment current base models apply unprompted — precisely the standard on which this library retired live-state-truth. Being Claude Code-only, it would also be dead weight on claude.ai. Nothing here earns per-session context cost or even on-demand shelf space.

Mostly routing and stop conditions a careful model would apply anyway; its real value is being the documented alternative branch in the writing-plans handoff and its self-deprecating pointer to the better subagent path. Every piece is already covered: plan-gate carries the same discipline with more teeth (pre-committed success criteria, per-phase predicted observations, STOP-and-re-diagnose at checkpoint mismatches), adversarial-verify owns the completion gate, Claude Code's plan mode and todo system handle the mechanics, and the Code harness itself now instructs branch-first on default branches. verdicts.name: finishing-a-development-branch

inv.name: finishing-a-development-branch
inv.description: Use when implementation is complete, all tests pass, and you need to decide how to integrate the work - guides completion of development work by presenting structured options for merge, PR, or cleanup
inv.purpose: Standardizes the end-of-branch workflow: verify tests, detect the workspace type, present an exact 4-option menu (merge locally / push+PR / keep / discard), execute the choice, and clean up worktrees only when provenance says Superpowers created them.
inv.key_content: Tests must pass before options are even presented. Environment detection via GIT_DIR vs GIT_COMMON (git rev-parse --git-dir vs --git-common-dir) distinguishing normal repo, named-branch worktree, and detached-HEAD (externally managed → reduced 3-option menu with no local merge). Exact scripted menus with 'don't add explanation'. Typed-'discard' confirmation before destroying work. Ordering rules encoding real failure modes: merge and verify BEFORE removing anything; remove worktree BEFORE git branch -d (else deletion fails); always cd to main repo root before git worktree remove (fails silently from inside); git worktree prune after removal. Provenance rule: only clean up worktrees under .worktrees/ or worktrees/ — harness-owned workspaces are never removed. Options 2 and 3 always preserve the worktree (PR iteration needs it). Quick-reference matrix and Never/Always red-flag lists.
inv.surfaces: Claude Code only — entirely git and shell driven.
inv.dependencies: git (worktree subcommands), project test runner; upstream skills executing-plans / subagent-driven-development invoke it; pairs with using-git-worktrees (which created the workspace).
inv.quality: Substantive and clearly hard-won: the GIT_DIR/GIT_COMMON detection, submodule-adjacent edge handling, deletion-ordering pitfalls, and harness-ownership provenance checks are exactly the details agents get wrong. Little padding. Current with modern harness-managed-worktree realities (detached HEAD path).

verdicts.overlaps_with: adversarial-verify (generic verify-before-delivering discipline covers the Step-1 test gate), Claude Code built-in verify (end-to-end change verification), Claude Code harness worktree tooling (EnterWorktree/ExitWorktree own harness-managed workspaces) and base-model git competence for plain merge/PR mechanics
verdicts.surfaces: Claude Code only — entirely git/shell driven; no claude.ai applicability
verdicts.license: MIT (Copyright 2025 Jesse Vincent, obra/superpowers)
verdicts.rationale: The genuinely net-new content is the worktree/branch teardown mechanics agents reliably get wrong: merge-and-verify BEFORE removing anything, remove worktree BEFORE git branch -d, cd to main repo root before git worktree remove (fails silently from inside), git worktree prune afterward, the provenance rule that only self-created .worktrees/ paths get cleaned while harness-owned workspaces are left alone, plus the exact 4-option integration menu and typed-'discard' confirmation. Nothing in the 14-skill core covers git integration workflow; the only core overlap is the thin Step-1 test gate already owned by adversarial-verify and the Claude Code verify built-in. But the skill is single-surface (dead weight on claude.ai, one of David's two equal surfaces) and its depth assumes a worktree-based branch workflow — standalone in a normal repo it reduces to a decision menu that base models plus Claude Code's existing git discipline handle adequately, and the harness's own EnterWorktree/ExitWorktree tools cover harness-managed workspaces. Verdict: real but situational — install project-scoped in repos where David actually runs branch/worktree development, not always-on. Upgrade to HIGHLY RECOMMEND only if its companion using-git-worktrees is also adopted, since the two form one workflow and most of this skill's hard-won ordering rules only fire in worktree-created workspaces.

verdicts.name: receiving-code-review

inv.name: receiving-code-review
inv.description: Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - requires technical rigor and verification, not performative agreement or blind implementation
inv.purpose: Anti-sycophancy discipline for handling review feedback: verify claims against the codebase before implementing, ask before assuming, push back with technical reasoning when the reviewer is wrong, and never perform gratitude or agreement. Treats external feedback as suggestions to evaluate, not orders.
inv.key_content: 6-step response pattern (READ → UNDERSTAND → VERIFY → EVALUATE → RESPOND → IMPLEMENT). Explicit forbidden-phrase list: 'You're absolutely right!', 'Great point!', ANY gratitude expression — with the rule 'if you catch yourself about to write Thanks: DELETE IT. State the fix instead.' All-or-nothing clarification rule: if items 4-5 of 6 are unclear, implement NOTHING until clarified (partial understanding = wrong implementation). Source-tiered trust: human partner (trusted, still no performative agreement) vs external reviewers (5-point verification checklist before implementing). YAGNI check: grep the codebase for actual usage before 'implementing properly'; quote — 'You and reviewer both report to me. If we don't need this feature, don't add it.' Implementation order: blocking → simple → complex, test each individually. Graceful-correction script for when your pushback was wrong (no long apology). GitHub-specific: reply in the comment thread via gh api .../comments/{id}/replies, not top-level.
inv.surfaces: Both — core behavioral discipline is surface-agnostic; only the gh CLI thread-reply detail and codebase-grep verification assume Claude Code.
inv.dependencies: None required (gh CLI optional for the GitHub thread detail). Complements requesting-code-review and subagent-driven-development's review loops.
inv.quality: Substantive behavioral engineering. The forbidden-phrase list, all-items-clear-first rule, and grep-before-implementing YAGNI check go beyond default model behavior (which still trends agreeable under review pressure). Slight overlap with newer models' anti-sycophancy training, but the concrete scripts keep it valuable. No staleness.

verdicts.overlaps_with: adversarial-verify (verify-don't-affirm ethos, but aimed at finished deliverables, not incoming feedback); scope-fence (the YAGNI check is per-scope approval with the expansion pressure coming from a reviewer); base-model anti-sycophancy training + built-in code-review/review (which cover GIVING review only)
verdicts.surfaces: Both — the core discipline (verify feedback claims before implementing, all-items-clear-first, no performative agreement) applies to any critique of any deliverable on claude.ai or Code; the codebase-grep YAGNI check and gh comment-thread-reply mechanic are Claude Code-only.
verdicts.license: MIT (Copyright 2025 Jesse Vincent, obra/superpowers)
verdicts.rationale: This owns a moment no core skill or built-in covers: evaluating INCOMING review feedback before acting on it — adversarial-verify fires when work looks done or an artifact needs grading, while built-in code-review/review/security-review only produce reviews, so the receiving direction is net-new. The durable capability is procedural, not tonal: the 5-point verify-reviewer-claims-against-this-codebase checklist, the all-or-nothing clarification rule (items 4-5 of 6 unclear → implement NOTHING, since partial understanding yields wrong implementations), blocking→simple→complex implementation order with per-item testing, and the grep-before-'implementing-properly' YAGNI check — all beyond default model behavior, which still trends toward implementing reviewer suggestions unverified. It is only HIGHLY RECOMMEND rather than MUST ADD because roughly a third of the body (the forbidden-phrase/no-gratitude list) overlaps modern anti-sycophancy training and would likely fail the same measured-redundancy standard that retired live-state-truth, and the YAGNI section duplicates scope-fence's per-scope-approval law. Recommend adopting after a skill-authoring rewrite: keep the verification procedure, clarification rule, and pushback/correction scripts; trim the tone-policing to one line; delegate reviewer-driven scope expansion to scope-fence by cross-reference. Always-on earns its one description line because feedback-on-work-in-flight recurs across most interactive sessions on both surfaces, and the discipline is worthless if it must be remembered and invoked at exactly the moment agreement pressure is highest.

verify.reasoning:
Read the candidate (scratchpad .../receiving-code-review/SKILL.md, 214 lines, single file) and core skills architecture-contract, adversarial-verify, scope-fence, plus skill-authoring's house-section requirements. The tier fails on all three checks in ways the analyst partially acknowledged but under-weighted.

(1) REDUNDANCY — the net-new claim is only fractionally true. Inventory of the body: (a) Forbidden Responses + Acknowledging Correct Feedback + Gracefully Correcting Your Pushback (~lines 27-38, 131-162, plus the Common Mistakes rows and Real Examples that restate them) are anti-sycophancy tone rules — 'never You're absolutely right / no gratitude / no long apology'. Modern Fable 5/Opus training targets exactly this phrase class; by the same measured-redundancy standard that RETIRE-CONFIRMED live-state-truth (architecture-contract Decision 7: zero behavioral delta in all eight cells because base models already do it), this material is presumptively inert, and it is closer to half the body than the analyst's 'roughly a third'. (b) The YAGNI section (lines 89-98) and 'reviewer suggests scope expansion' cases are scope-fence's per-scope-approval law verbatim in spirit (scope-fence rules 1, 4; its description already covers requests that bundle a fix with vague expansion). (c) The 5-point external-reviewer checklist (lines 68-84) is adversarial-verify's ethos pointed at an incoming claim rather than an outgoing deliverable — adversarial-verify's description already covers 'the user hands you something and asks is this right'; a reviewer comment is such an artifact. The genuinely uncovered residue is small: the all-or-nothing clarification rule (unclear on 4,5 of 6 → implement nothing), the blocking→simple→complex order with per-item testing (which strong models largely do by default), and a one-line gh-api thread-reply tip. That residue is real but thin — closer to one wording clause added to adversarial-verify's trigger ('incoming review feedback before acting on it') than to a standalone always-on skill.

(2) ARCHITECTURE — the proposed placement ('always-on personal') collides with the library's freshest, hardest-won decision. Decision 5 as amended by Decision 7 (2026-07-11, two days before today) deliberately shrank the personal always-on set to exactly three governors after measurement showed always-on context cost buying no behavior change; adding a fourth, unmeasured, externally-sourced always-on skill reverses that on zero evidence, and weak-point 1 plus governance-adoption-campaign say additions go through trigger evals first. The candidate as written also violates Decision 2 (procedure text saturated with 'your human partner's rule' — source-repo-specific social context), Decision 3 (description has no NOT clause at all), and lacks every required house section (no When-NOT, no Provenance, no dated volatile facts — skill-authoring Axis 4). The analyst's tier is conditional on a full rewrite, which concedes the artifact itself is not adoptable; a recommendation whose deliverable is 'write a different, smaller skill and get it past the campaign' is not HIGHLY RECOMMEND for this candidate.

(3) SUBSTANCE — mixed, and the strongest parts are unproven. The all-or-nothing clarification rule is the one rule that genuinely contradicts default model behavior (default: implement the clear items, ask about the rest) — but it is also the most debatable rule in the file (partial implementation of independent items is often correct), and no measurement supports it. The 5-point checklist and implementation order are procedural but close to what a strong model does when it has the codebase in front of it; the tone-policing half would be retired by the library's own measurement standard. Under 'when torn, choose lower' and given the retirement precedent, the correct tier is NICE TO HAVE: worth mining for a one-clause extension to adversarial-verify's description plus possibly the clarification rule, not worth adopting as a fourth always-on governor.

verdicts.name: requesting-code-review

inv.name: requesting-code-review
inv.description: Use when completing tasks, implementing major features, or before merging to verify work meets requirements
inv.purpose: Procedure for dispatching a fresh code-reviewer subagent with precisely scoped context (never the session's history): capture BASE/HEAD SHAs, fill the code-reviewer.md template, and act on findings by severity. Positions review as mandatory after each task and before merge.
inv.key_content: Core principle 'review early, review often' with mandatory triggers (after each task in SDD, after major features, before merge) and optional ones (when stuck, before refactoring). Exact SHA-capture commands (BASE_SHA=$(git rev-parse HEAD~1) or origin/main). Template placeholder contract: DESCRIPTION, PLAN_OR_REQUIREMENTS, BASE_SHA, HEAD_SHA. Severity-tiered response policy: fix Critical immediately, Important before proceeding, note Minor; push back with reasoning if reviewer is wrong. The companion code-reviewer.md template (172 lines) contains the real substance: a Senior Code Reviewer persona, read-only review contract (never mutate the working tree/HEAD; use git worktree add into /tmp for other revisions), and plan-alignment checks.

verdicts.name: subagent-driven-development

inv.name: subagent-driven-development
inv.description: Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks in the current session
inv.purpose: The flagship execution engine: a controller loop dispatching a fresh implementer subagent per plan task, a task reviewer (spec compliance + code quality) after each, fix subagents for findings, and one whole-branch review at the end — all engineered to keep the controller's context small and to survive compaction. Encodes the most operational hard-won knowledge in the repo.
inv.key_content: Continuous-execution rule (no 'should I continue?' check-ins; stop only on BLOCKED/ambiguity/done). Model-tier selection economics: cheapest tier only for transcription-grade tasks, mid-tier floor for reviewers ('turn count beats token price' — cheap models take 2-3x turns), most-capable model mandated for the final whole-branch review, and ALWAYS specify the model explicitly (omitted model silently inherits the expensive session model). 4-status implementer protocol (DONE / DONE_WITH_CONCERNS / NEEDS_CONTEXT / BLOCKED) with escalation ladder — never force the same model to retry unchanged. File-handoff discipline via bundled scripts: task-brief PLAN_FILE N extracts one task to a file; review-package BASE HEAD writes commit list + stat + full diff to a file (with the trap warning: BASE is the recorded pre-dispatch commit, NEVER HEAD~1 which silently drops multi-commit work); reports named after briefs. Reviewer-integrity rules: never tell a reviewer what not to flag or pre-rate severity ('if your prompt contains "do not flag"... stop: you are pre-judging'); plan-mandated findings go to the human. Durable progress ledger at .superpowers/sdd/progress.md because 'controllers that lost their place have re-dispatched entire completed task sequences — the single most expensive failure observed'; trust ledger + git log over memory after compaction. One fix subagent for ALL final-review findings (a real per-finding fix wave cost more than all tasks combined). Anti-pattern citation: a 42k-char dispatch that was 99% pasted history. Pre-flight plan-conflict scan batched into one question.
inv.surfaces: Claude Code only — requires subagents, bash scripts, git, and a plan file; the deepest harness coupling in the repo.
inv.dependencies: scripts/task-brief, scripts/review-package, scripts/sdd-workspace (bundled); implementer-prompt.md and task-reviewer-prompt.md templates; requesting-code-review's code-reviewer.md for the final review; writing-plans (input), using-git-worktrees (workspace), finishing-a-development-branch (exit), test-driven-development (subagent discipline).
inv.quality: The crown jewel — extremely substantive, with named cost figures, exact failure anecdotes, and non-obvious traps (HEAD~1 truncation, model inheritance, ledger-vs-memory) that no base model produces unprompted. Long (~420 lines), but nearly all of it is load-bearing. Current as of 2026-era practices (compaction recovery, model-tier pricing).

verdicts.overlaps_with: Claude Code built-ins: subagents/Agent tool + Workflow orchestration (raw dispatch mechanism), code-review (its final whole-branch review role), verify (per-task behavioral checking). Core library: tangential only — plan-gate (upstream planning, explicitly not execution) and adversarial-verify (deliverable grading; SDD's reviewer loop echoes the spirit but shares no mechanics). No core skill covers execution orchestration.

Best kept as explicit invocation (or copied project-scoped into active dev repos), since its trigger moment ("execute this plan") is always user-initiated and it earns nothing on claude.ai.

verdicts.rationale: Verified against the actual files: the built-ins provide only the dispatch mechanism, while this skill supplies the operational discipline nothing else in David's stack has — explicit model-tier economics ("turn count beats token price"; omitted model silently inherits the expensive session model), the review-package BASE-never-HEAD~1 trap, file-handoff scripts that keep bulk artifacts out of controller context, a 4-status implementer protocol with an escalation ladder, reviewer-integrity rules against pre-judging findings, and a compaction-survivable progress ledger motivated by the "single most expensive failure observed" (re-dispatching completed tasks). This is exactly the anecdote-backed, non-obvious knowledge that survives the library's live-state-truth retirement standard — no base model produces it unprompted. It stops short of MUST ADD on three grounds matching the HIGHLY RECOMMEND definition verbatim: partial overlap with /code-review and /verify for its review roles, Claude Code-only reach (half of David's equally-weighted surfaces), and the repo's heaviest dependency web — its final-review step references ../requesting-code-review/code-reviewer.md and it integrates with writing-plans, finishing-a-development-branch, using-git-worktrees, and test-driven-development, so standalone adoption requires adapting those references or dispositioning the companions together. If the companion skills are also adopted, this is the flagship of the suite and the strongest single execution-quality upgrade available for David's Claude Code surface; on-demand placement keeps its ~22KB body plus templates out of every session's context.

verify.reasoning: REDUNDANCY — verified against actual files; net-new claim is TRUE. I confirmed every load-bearing claim in the analyst's record against the candidate's SKILL.md: model-omission trap ("An omitted model inherits your session's model — often the most capable and most expensive", lines 115-117) and "Turn count beats token price" (line 119); the BASE-never-HEAD~1 rule appears twice (lines 136, 186-188) and is enforced in scripts/review-package (header comment + per-range file naming); the 4-status implementer protocol with escalation ladder (lines 132-148, mirrored in implementer-prompt.md's "When You're in Over Your Head"); reviewer-integrity anti-pre-judging rules with concrete tells ("do not flag", "at most Minor", lines 168-173); anecdote-backed rules a base model would not produce (42k-char dispatch of 99% pasted history, line 191-193; final-review fix wave costing more than all tasks combined, lines 214-217; ledger motivated by "the single most expensive failure observed" — re-dispatching completed tasks after compaction, lines 249-264). No core skill covers execution orchestration: plan-gate is explicitly upstream planning, adversarial-verify grades finished deliverables and shares no mechanics with SDD's per-task loop, and the other 12 core skills (scope-fence, lessons-ledger, skill-authoring, etc.) are unrelated. Built-ins overlap only at the edges: subagents/Workflow provide raw dispatch with zero economics or handoff discipline; /code-review and /verify can substitute for SDD's review ROLES but not the controller protocol. Applying the live-state-truth retirement standard: that skill was retired because base models already check live state natively; base models dispatching subagents do NOT by default record BASE commits, extract task briefs to files, downshift model tiers, or keep a compaction-survivable ledger — so measurement would not retire this. ARCHITECTURE — no invariant broken at on-demand placement. Checked all 8 invariants in /home/user/claude-core-skills/.claude/skills/architecture-contract/SKILL.md: Decision 5's context-cost law is satisfied by on-demand placement (22KB body + 13KB templates stay out of always-on context); anti-ceremony (Decision 4) is respected — SDD gates only on "have implementation plan with independent tasks" and its own Continuous Execution rule bans check-in ceremony; placement law is trivially satisfiable. Real but non-blocking adaptation debt: the current one-line description ("Use when executing implementation plans...") fails Decision 3 (no quoted triggers, no NOT clause) and there is no Provenance section — required rework under skill-authoring, but adoption-cost, not an invariant break. SUBSTANCE — decisively substantive. Two runnable scripts (review-package builds commit-list+stat+U10-diff packages with per-range filenames so re-reviews get fresh files; task-brief awk-extracts one task with code-fence awareness), two full dispatch templates with a report contract (report file vs. under-15-line return), and rules that would measurably change a strong model's behavior: Fable 5/Opus orchestrating naturally pastes task text into prompts, inherits the expensive session model, uses HEAD~1 for diffs, and loses its place after compaction. This is not be-careful prose. Refutation angles that failed: (a) "dependency web makes it unusable standalone" — the dependencies are at entry/exit (writing-plans upstream, finishing-a-development-branch and ../requesting-code-review/code-reviewer.md at the end); the core loop (brief → implementer → review-package → task-reviewer → ledger) is self-contained in this directory, and built-in /code-review can stand in for the final review; (b) "Claude Code Workflow built-in now covers orchestration" — it covers dispatch mechanics only, none of the economics/handoff/ledger discipline. The caveats that keep it below MUST ADD (Claude Code-only reach, partial review-role overlap with built-ins, heaviest dependency web in the repo, description rework required) are exactly the ones the analyst already priced in, and they match the HIGHLY RECOMMEND definition rather than NICE TO HAVE — the tier is calibrated, not inflated. Upheld.

verdicts.name: systematic-debugging

inv.name: systematic-debugging
inv.description: Use when encountering any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior, before proposing fixes
inv.purpose: A four-phase gate forcing root-cause investigation before any fix attempt, with an escalation rule that three failed fixes means the architecture — not the hypothesis — is wrong. Designed to hold under exactly the pressures (emergencies, 'obvious' quick fixes) that make agents guess.
inv.key_content: Iron Law: 'NO FIXES WITHOUT ROOT CAUSE INVESTIGATION FIRST' plus 'violating the letter is violating the spirit'. Phase 1: read errors completely, reproduce, check recent changes, and — distinctively — instrument EVERY component boundary in multi-layer systems (worked CI→build→signing example logging what enters/exits each layer) before proposing anything. Phase 3: single written hypothesis, smallest possible test, one variable. Phase 4: failing test required before the fix (via TDD skill); fix counter with hard stop — under 3 failures return to Phase 1, at 3+ STOP and question the architecture with the human ('this is NOT a failed hypothesis - this is a wrong architecture'). Red-flag thought list ('just try changing X', 'it's probably X') and a rationalization table (emergency/simple-issue excuses). A 'your human partner's signals you're doing it wrong' table decoding phrases like 'Is that not happening?' and 'Stop guessing'. Companion docs: root-cause-tracing.md (backward stack tracing), defense-in-depth.md (multi-layer validation after root cause), condition-based-waiting.md + example.ts (replace timeouts with condition polling), find-polluter.sh (bisect test pollution).
inv.surfaces: Both — the methodology is surface-agnostic; instrumentation examples and companion scripts assume bash/Claude Code.
inv.dependencies: Companion files in the same directory (root-cause-tracing.md, defense-in-depth.md, condition-based-waiting.md/.ts, find-polluter.sh); chains to test-driven-development (Phase 4 failing test) and verification-before-completion. Directory also carries its own pressure-test scenarios (test-pressure-1..3.md, test-academic.md) and CREATION-LOG.md.
inv.quality: Substantive. The 3-strikes-question-the-architecture rule, boundary instrumentation recipe, and human-signal decoding table exceed default behavior. The 'Real-World Impact' stats (95% vs 40% first-fix rate) are unverifiable motivational numbers. The shipped pressure-test files are a rare, honest look at how the skill was validated.

verdicts.overlaps_with: plan-gate (partial: hypothesis/assumption discipline and the 'figure out why...' trigger); adversarial-verify + Claude Code built-in `verify` (post-fix verification slice only); base-model debugging basics (Phase 1's read-errors/reproduce/check-recent-changes). debugging-playbook is name-adjacent but domain-disjoint — it covers Claude session/skill failures only, not code debugging.
verdicts.surfaces: Both, equally usable — the four-phase methodology is surface-agnostic and works in claude.ai code/analysis sessions; the companion payloads (find-polluter.sh bisection, bash boundary-instrumentation examples, condition-based-waiting.ts) execute only in Claude Code, making it the primary surface.
verdicts.license: MIT (Copyright 2025 Jesse Vincent, superpowers repo LICENSE)
verdicts.rationale: The core library has no general software-debugging methodology at all — debugging-playbook is strictly about Claude session/skill failures, so the only real overlaps are plan-gate's generic hypothesis discipline and adversarial-verify/`verify` on the post-fix slice. What this adds is concrete enforcement mechanics base models demonstrably lack under pressure: the no-fixes-before-root-cause gate, a fix counter with a hard stop at 3 failures that escalates to questioning the architecture with the human, a per-component-boundary instrumentation recipe for multi-layer systems, and rationalization/red-flag tables built to hold during exactly the emergencies that make agents guess-and-patch — this survives the live-state-truth retirement standard because it is mechanism, not think-carefully prose. Standout companions: root-cause-tracing.md (backward stack tracing to the original trigger), defense-in-depth.md (4-layer validation after root cause), and the runnable find-polluter.sh test-pollution bisector. It is HIGHLY RECOMMEND rather than MUST ADD because of the partial plan-gate/adversarial-verify overlap and needed adaptation work: strip the unverifiable 95%-vs-40% impact stats, rewrite the description to house style with a NOT clause disambiguating it from debugging-playbook, and rewire the superpowers:verification-before-completion reference to adversarial-verify (and decide what replaces the TDD-skill dependency in Phase 4). Placement is always-on personal because the trigger cost is one description line while the value depends entirely on firing before the agent starts thrashing — a debugging discipline invoked on demand is self-defeating, since the failure mode is not realizing you are in it.

verify.reasoning: REDUNDANCY: Overlap is larger than the analyst recorded. plan-gate (/home/user/claude-core-skills/.claude/skills/plan-gate/SKILL.md) triggers on "figure out why..." and already mandates hypothesis/assumption registers, predicted observations per phase, and surprise->STOP-re-diagnose — covering most of the candidate's Phases 1 and 3; adversarial-verify plus the built-in `verify` skill covers Phase 4's behavioral verification and regression blocking. The genuinely net-new mechanics in the candidate (scratchpad .../systematic-debugging/SKILL.md and companions) reduce to the fix-counter hard stop at 3 with architecture escalation, the per-component-boundary instrumentation recipe, find-polluter.sh, and defense-in-depth's 4-layer pattern. The analyst's key claim that base models "demonstrably lack" this under pressure is unmeasured on the current model class — and this library's own retirement precedent (architecture-contract Decision 7: live-state-truth RETIRE-CONFIRMED, zero behavioral delta in all eight cells because base models already check live state) demands measurement, not plausibility, before crediting that claim. ARCHITECTURE: The proposed always-on personal placement breaks the contract on three counts. (1) Decision 7/Decision 5: the owner just retired two always-on governors on measured context-cost grounds, shrinking the active set to three; adding a fourth always-on skill, unmeasured and external, contradicts that freshly executed discipline — admission should be gated by governance-adoption-campaign's A/B pipeline (weak point 1: even surviving governors have zero trigger evals). (2) Decision 4/Invariant 5 (anti-ceremony): the candidate's body affirmatively rejects proportionality ("Use for ANY technical issue", "Don't skip when: Issue seems simple", "Violating the letter of this process is violating the spirit") — every house governor carries a triage rule as first-class law, so the required rewrite is structural, not the cosmetic adaptation the analyst scoped. (3) Decision 3/Invariant 1: one-line description with no quoted triggers or NOT clause, plus unverifiable stats (95% vs 40%, "95% of no-root-cause cases") and dangling superpowers:test-driven-development / verification-before-completion dependencies. SUBSTANCE: Mixed — the fix-counter escalation, boundary instrumentation, bisection script, and defense-in-depth files are real mechanism, but much of the body (Iron Law, red-flag list, rationalization table, "human partner signals") is emphatic be-careful prose of exactly the genre this library's measurement retires, and Phases 1-3 largely restate base-model behavior plus plan-gate law. Net: the library truly lacks a general software-debugging methodology and the novel slice is worth harvesting, so not NOT NEEDED; but HIGHLY RECOMMEND at always-on placement fails the architecture check and rests on an unmeasured behavioral claim. Torn between tiers, the lower wins: NICE TO HAVE — harvest the net-new mechanics as a candidate and admit always-on only after fresh-session A/B measurement (research-methodology, N=2 floor) shows a delta live-state-truth failed to show.

verdicts.name: test-driven-development

inv.name: test-driven-development
inv.description: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code
inv.purpose: Enforces strict red-green-refactor with the most radical rule in the repo: code written before its test must be DELETED, not adapted or kept as reference. Most of the body is pre-emptive demolition of every known rationalization for testing after.
inv.key_content: Iron Law: 'NO PRODUCTION CODE WITHOUT A FAILING TEST FIRST'; delete-means-delete with explicit loophole closures (don't keep as 'reference', don't 'adapt' it, don't look at it). Mandatory verify-RED step — watch the test fail for the RIGHT reason (feature missing, not typo); a test that passes immediately means you're testing existing behavior. Good/Bad paired TypeScript examples (real-behavior retry test vs mock-testing test; minimal implementation vs YAGNI-violating options object). 'Why Order Matters' essays rebutting five specific arguments verbatim ('tests after achieve the same goals — it's spirit not ritual' → 'Tests-after answer what does this do? Tests-first answer what should this do?'; sunk-cost deletion math). 11-row rationalization table and 13-item red-flag list ('This is different because...'). Verification checklist gating completion. When-stuck table (must-mock-everything → too coupled, use DI). Pointer to testing-anti-patterns.md (299 lines: testing mock behavior, test-only methods in production classes).
inv.surfaces: Both in principle (discipline applies anywhere code is written) but practically Claude Code — requires running test commands to observe red/green.
inv.dependencies: A test runner in the project; testing-anti-patterns.md companion; invoked by systematic-debugging Phase 4 and expected of SDD implementer subagents; writing-skills declares it required background.
inv.quality: Highly substantive as behavior enforcement. Any strong model knows TDD; almost none enforce delete-and-restart or verify-red under pressure without this scaffolding. The rationalization-rebuttal essays are the distinctive asset — they were reportedly built from real baseline-test transcripts. Opinionated to the point of dogma (deleting hours of work), which is a deliberate, stated trade-off.

verdicts.overlaps_with: adversarial-verify (post-hoc evidence/behavioral verification of finished work); Claude Code built-ins verify and code-review (post-hoc exercise/review of a completed diff); base-model TDD knowledge (mechanics only, not under-pressure enforcement). None cover test-first ordering, verify-RED, or delete-pre-test-code.
verdicts.surfaces: Claude Code only in practice — requires executing a test runner against a repo to observe red/green; on claude.ai it is dead per-session weight since a project test suite cannot be run there.
verdicts.license: MIT (obra/superpowers, Copyright 2025 Jesse Vincent) — verified in repo LICENSE file.
verdicts.rationale: Verified against the actual SKILL.md: the distinctive asset is enforcement of test-BEFORE-code ordering — the Iron Law, mandatory verify-RED (watch the test fail for the right reason), delete-means-delete with explicit loophole closures, and an 11-row rationalization-rebuttal table built for exactly the moments models cave. Everything in the core library and built-ins (adversarial-verify, Code's verify/code-review) is post-hoc — it checks work after it exists — so the ordering discipline is genuinely net-new, while the verification-side content (watch it pass, pristine output) partially duplicates adversarial-verify, hence overlap rather than net-new. It fails the MUST ADD bar on two grounds: it is single-surface in practice (needs a runnable test suite, so zero claude.ai value despite equal surface weighting), and its any-feature-any-bugfix trigger plus delete-hours-of-work dogma conflicts with the library's anti-ceremony invariant if installed globally — wrong for throwaway scripts, prototypes, and personal-ops automation. Placed project-scoped it auto-loads only in repos where David opts into strict TDD, which matches the strict-tiered footprint and the skill's own dependency on project test infrastructure. If adopted, the description should be rewritten to house style (skill-authoring NOT-clause contract), and the companion testing-anti-patterns.md ships with it; references to superpowers siblings (systematic-debugging) are non-blocking pointers.

verify.reasoning: Redundancy: the core net-new claim partially survives — nothing post-hoc in the library covers test-first ordering (adversarial-verify/SKILL.md is explicitly the pass after work looks done; built-ins verify/code-review exercise completed diffs). But the net-new fraction is only half the skill: Verify-GREEN (SKILL.md lines 168-183: watch it pass, pristine output, other tests green) duplicates adversarial-verify §2 and the built-in verify skill, and testing-anti-patterns.md (mock hygiene, no test-only production methods) is base-model knowledge plus code-review territory. Applying the library's own retirement standard (architecture-contract Decision 7: live-state-truth RETIRE-CONFIRMED because base models already did the behavior), the fair baseline is a one-line project directive "this repo uses strict TDD" — a strong model so instructed executes red-green-refactor; the skill's only delta is the anti-rationalization enforcement content, which is plausible but unmeasured, exactly the claim class this library labels candidate-not-fact (weak-point 1). Architecture: as shipped the skill breaks Decision 3 (description "Use when implementing any feature or bugfix" is an always-trigger with no NOT clause — the pattern Decision 1 says triggers never or always) and Decision 4/invariant 5 (anti-ceremony: body mandates TDD for every bugfix/refactor, exceptions need human permission, delete-hours-of-work). The analyst's project-scoped-plus-rewrite mitigation is coherent under Decision 2, but it means the tier was awarded to a hypothetical rewritten artifact, not the one on disk. Substance: passes — the body is directive and checkable (verify-RED with expected failure reason, delete-and-reimplement, 11-row rationalization table), not be-careful prose, and test-first is genuinely non-default model behavior. Net ruling: a single-surface (needs a runnable test suite, zero claude.ai value), opt-in (valuable only in repos where David elects strict TDD — none evidenced), needs-full-rewrite-before-install, unmeasured-enforcement skill whose unique content is one half of its body is NICE TO HAVE, not HIGHLY RECOMMEND. Per the instruction, when torn between tiers choose the lower.

verdicts.name: using-git-worktrees

inv.name: using-git-worktrees
inv.description: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - ensures an isolated workspace exists via native tools or git worktree fallback
inv.purpose: Ensures feature work happens in an isolated workspace using a strict detection-first cascade: detect existing isolation, prefer the harness's native worktree tool, and only fall back to manual git worktree — then run project setup and verify a clean test baseline.
inv.key_content: Step 0 detection via GIT_DIR vs GIT_COMMON with a submodule guard (git rev-parse --show-superproject-working-tree — submodules also have GIT_DIR != GIT_COMMON and must be treated as normal repos). Consent gate before creating a worktree unless the user pre-declared a preference. Step 1a: 'never fight the harness' — look for native tools (EnterWorktree, /worktree, --worktree) first; using git worktree add when a native tool exists 'creates phantom state your harness can't see' and is called the #1 mistake. Step 1b fallback with directory priority (declared preference > existing .worktrees/ > worktrees/ > default .worktrees/) and a MANDATORY git check-ignore verification before creating (add to .gitignore and commit if missing). Sandbox fallback: on permission denial, tell the user and work in place. Steps 2-3: auto-detect setup (npm/cargo/pip/poetry/go) and run baseline tests, asking before proceeding on failures. Quick-reference situation table.
inv.surfaces: Claude Code only — pure git/shell, and its native-tool logic references Code-style tools like EnterWorktree.
inv.dependencies: git worktrees; optional native harness worktree tools; project package managers for setup; invoked by executing-plans/SDD and undone by finishing-a-development-branch.
inv.quality: Substantive: the submodule guard, check-ignore requirement, native-tool-first doctrine, and sandbox fallback are concrete traps a base model routinely misses. Current with 2026 harness-managed-workspace behavior. Minimal padding.

verdicts.overlaps_with: Claude Code built-in native worktree tooling (EnterWorktree/ExitWorktree, --worktree/cloud-session isolation); no overlap with any core library skill (grep for worktree/isolation across all 14 SKILL.md files: zero hits)
verdicts.surfaces: Claude Code only — pure git/shell procedure plus Code-style native-tool references (EnterWorktree); zero value on claude.ai, which is half of David's equally-weighted surface mix
verdicts.license: MIT (Copyright 2025 Jesse Vincent, obra/superpowers)
verdicts.rationale: The skill is genuinely well-built: the submodule guard (GIT_DIR != GIT_COMMON is also true in submodules), the mandatory git check-ignore before creating a project-local worktree, and the "never git-worktree-add when a native tool exists — it creates phantom state the harness can't see" doctrine are concrete traps base models do hit. But on David's only applicable surface, Claude Code, the harness already ships EnterWorktree/ExitWorktree and increasingly auto-isolates sessions, so the skill's primary path collapses to "use the built-in tool," and its manual-fallback depth mainly serves non-Claude harnesses David doesn't run. It is also designed as one link in superpowers' larger chain (invoked by executing-plans, closed out by finishing-a-development-branch), so standalone it loses part of its workflow value. Residual net-new value — submodule guard, check-ignore discipline, clean-baseline test verification before feature work — is real but situational and Code-only, which under the strict-tiered footprint does not justify always-on per-session cost on both surfaces. Keep it as an on-demand install (or drop it into a specific repo as project-scoped) for sessions that do multi-branch parallel feature work; do not add to the always-on personal set.
