verdicts.name: ComposioHQ/awesome-claude-skills (file-organizer, invoice-organizer, meeting-insights-analyzer, content-research-writer, video-downloader, image-enhancer)

inv.name: ComposioHQ/awesome-claude-skills (file-organizer, invoice-organizer, meeting-insights-analyzer, content-research-writer, video-downloader, image-enhancer)
inv.what_it_is: A community "awesome list" style monorepo from Composio that mixes (a) ~20 hand-written community skills, (b) verbatim copies of Anthropic's official skills (canvas-design, mcp-builder, skill-creator, etc.), and (c) 832 auto-generated "composio-skills/*-automation" wrapper directories for Composio's own tool catalog. The six shortlisted skills are standalone SKILL.md prompt-playbooks for personal-ops tasks: five of them are pure prose workflows (organize files/invoices with bash + Claude's file reading, analyze meeting transcripts for communication patterns, run a research-outline-draft-cite writing loop, "enhance" images) and one (video-downloader) ships an actual 144-line Python CLI wrapping yt-dlp. Each prose skill follows the same template: When-to-Use bullets, a numbered Instructions section with bash snippets and markdown output templates, worked examples attributed to named practitioners (Dan Shipper, Lenny Rachitsky), and Pro Tips. They are best understood as structured prompt scaffolding that shapes Claude's interaction pattern (ask scope questions, propose a plan, confirm before moving/deleting, emit a summary/CSV) rather than as code that adds capability.
inv.skills_inside: Collection; the six shortlisted: (1) file-organizer (434 lines, prose-only) — analyze a directory, find duplicates, propose a folder structure, confirm, then mv/mkdir with logging; good confirm-before-delete discipline but its duplicate-detection bash is broken as written (macOS `md5` piped to `sort | uniq -d` can never match because each line contains the unique file path, and it mixes macOS-only `md5` with GNU-only `find -printf`). (2) invoice-organizer (446 lines, prose-only) — read PDFs/receipt images, extract vendor/date/amount, rename to "YYYY-MM-DD Vendor - Invoice - Desc.pdf", sort into year/category folders, emit an accountant CSV; the most genuinely useful of the set, entirely reliant on Claude's native document reading. (3) meeting-insights-analyzer (327 lines, prose-only) — scan transcript files (.txt/.vtt/.srt), detect conflict-avoidance/hedging, speaking ratios, filler words, active listening, and output timestamped quoted examples plus a stats summary. (4) content-research-writer (538 lines, prose-only) — collaborative outline → research with citations → hook alternatives → section-by-section feedback templates → voice-preservation rules; a solid interaction script but its "research" step just says "search for relevant information" with no tooling. (5) video-downloader (98-line SKILL.md + scripts/download_video.py, 144 lines) — the only skill with real code: argparse CLI over yt-dlp with quality/format/audio-only flags, self-installs yt-dlp via pip; note the frontmatter name is "youtube-downloader", mismatching its directory name. (6) image-enhancer (99 lines) — the weakest: pure aspirational prose ("Upscaled to 2560x1440, sharpened edges") with zero implementation — no script, no ImageMagick/PIL commands, nothing telling Claude HOW to enhance an image.
inv.quality: Mixed, mostly mediocre. The prose skills are heavily padded (repeated example-output blocks, emoji "Organization Complete!" templates, Pro Tips sections) and largely encode interaction etiquette — ask scope questions, show a plan, confirm before destructive ops, emit a CSV/summary — that a strong base model does unprompted or with one sentence of instruction; there is little hard-won domain knowledge. Concrete defects: file-organizer's duplicate-finding pipeline is non-functional as written and platform-inconsistent; image-enhancer has no procedure at all; video-downloader's frontmatter name mismatches its directory (can affect discovery) and hardcodes the claude.ai container path /mnt/user-data/outputs as default. The naming conventions (YYYY-MM-DD prefixes, invoice filename schema, the invoice CSV schema) and the meeting-analysis rubric (hedging-language markers, speaking-ratio metrics) are the only genuinely reusable encoded artifacts. Maintenance: repo is active (last commit 2026-05-22, ~7 weeks before evaluation; PR #880 suggests high contribution volume), but activity is dominated by the auto-generated composio-skills bulk and list curation, not by hardening these six skills. Verdict: invoice-organizer and video-downloader are worth borrowing ideas/code from; meeting-insights-analyzer and content-research-writer are decent interaction templates; file-organizer needs its bash fixed; image-enhancer is an empty shell.
inv.dependencies: Five of six need only a shell with coreutils plus Claude's built-in PDF/image reading — no APIs, no MCP servers. Caveats: file-organizer's snippets assume macOS (`md5`) and GNU findutils (`-printf`) simultaneously, so some commands fail on any single platform. video-downloader requires python3, network access to YouTube, yt-dlp (self-installed via `pip install --break-system-packages`), and implicitly ffmpeg for stream merging (never mentioned or installed — a latent failure on bare systems). content-research-writer's research step implicitly assumes web search capability. invoice-organizer/image-enhancer batch work implies local filesystem access. Nothing uses Composio's own platform despite the repo owner.
inv.surfaces: Effectively both, skewing Claude Code. All six are plain SKILL.md files installable in Claude Code, and their instructions assume a local filesystem and bash ("cd ~", mkdir/mv), which is Claude Code/Cowork territory — file-organizer and invoice-organizer are meaningless without filesystem access. meeting-insights-analyzer and content-research-writer would work on claude.ai with uploaded transcripts/drafts if packaged as .skill. video-downloader is ironically the most claude.ai-oriented (default output /mnt/user-data/outputs is the claude.ai code-execution container path) but YouTube network access is typically blocked there, so in practice it too runs best in Claude Code.
inv.license: Apache-2.0 (declared in README badge and License section; no LICENSE file at repo root). README warns individual skills may carry their own licenses — the copied Anthropic skills have their own LICENSE.txt, but none of the six shortlisted skills do, so Apache-2.0 applies to them.

verdicts.overlaps_with: Base-model behavior (file organization, confirm-before-destructive-ops etiquette, transcript analysis, drafting loops — the exact class of prose that got live-state-truth retired); Claude's native PDF/image reading (all of invoice-organizer's extraction); claude.ai research mode + deep-research built-in (content-research-writer's research step); base-model bash/yt-dlp one-liners (video-downloader). No overlap with any of the 14 core governance skills.
verdicts.surfaces: Claude Code/Cowork primarily (file-organizer, invoice-organizer, video-downloader require local filesystem/bash; video-downloader additionally needs YouTube network access, typically blocked on claude.ai); meeting-insights-analyzer and content-research-writer would also run on claude.ai with uploaded files.
verdicts.license: Apache-2.0 (repo-level, declared in README badge and License section; no LICENSE file at root and no per-skill licenses on the six shortlisted skills, so Apache-2.0 governs them)
verdicts.rationale: Verified by reading the files: five of the six are 300-550-line prose playbooks whose bodies encode interaction etiquette (ask scope, propose plan, confirm before mv/rm, emit CSV/summary) that current base models perform unprompted — precisely the dead-weight class this library retired live-state-truth over, so installing them would contradict the strict-tiered footprint's own measured standard. The best defensible sub-skill, invoice-organizer, reduces to one reusable artifact (the "YYYY-MM-DD Vendor - Invoice - Desc.pdf" schema plus a CSV column list) that fits in one sentence of prompt, with all real work done by Claude's native document reading. Concrete defects confirmed in the source: file-organizer's duplicate-detection bash (SKILL.md line 135) is non-functional on every platform and mixes macOS-only md5 with GNU-only find -printf; image-enhancer has literally no procedure, only fabricated example output; video-downloader's frontmatter name (youtube-downloader) mismatches its directory and it silently depends on unmentioned ffmpeg. If David ever wants the invoice naming convention or meeting-insights-analyzer's hedging/speaking-ratio rubric, the right move under skill-authoring house style is to borrow those few lines into a tight self-authored on-demand skill, not to install any of these six.

verdicts.name: Trail of Bits Security Skills

inv.name: Trail of Bits Security Skills (trailofbits/skills)
inv.what_it_is: A Claude Code plugin marketplace from Trail of Bits (a top-tier security consultancy) containing 40 plugins wrapping 75 skills for AI-assisted security work. The core is professional audit workflow: orchestrated CodeQL and Semgrep scanning with SARIF merging, variant analysis (turning one found bug into a codebase-wide hunt), differential security review of PRs/diffs, false-positive verification with mandatory gate reviews, and language-specific deep reviews (C/C++, Rust) that spawn clustered parallel subagent workers and emit SARIF. Around that core sit a full fuzzing curriculum (ported from their public Testing Handbook at appsec.guide), smart-contract vulnerability scanners for 6 blockchains, crypto-specific verification (constant-time analysis, zeroize audits, ProVerif model generation), YARA authoring, supply-chain auditing, and a handful of dev-workflow utilities. It installs via `/plugin marketplace add trailofbits/skills` and is also consumable by OpenAI Codex through Claude-marketplace compatibility. It has a "Trophy Case" documenting a real timing side-channel bug in RustCrypto ML-DSA signing found using these skills.
inv.skills_inside: Collection: 40 plugins / 75 skills. By plugin — static-analysis: codeql (full CodeQL workflow: DB quality checks, custom .qls suites, data-extension modeling), semgrep (parallel-subagent scans, Pro detection, third-party rulesets, --metrics=off enforced), sarif-parsing (aggregate/dedupe scanner output). variant-analysis: variant-analysis (5-step pattern generalization from a known bug to CodeQL/Semgrep queries). differential-review: differential-review (risk-first PR/diff security review with blast-radius calc and git-history baselining). fp-check: fp-check (systematic TRUE/FALSE-POSITIVE verdicts with data-flow tracing, bug-class-specific checklists, devil's-advocate gate). c-review and rust-review: comprehensive per-language security reviews with parallel workers, tested Python SARIF-generation scripts. semgrep-rule-creator / semgrep-rule-variant-creator: author and port Semgrep rules test-driven. audit-context-building: line-by-line architectural context building pre-audit. insecure-defaults, sharp-edges, supply-chain-risk-auditor, agentic-actions-auditor (GitHub Actions AI-agent workflow audits), dimensional-analysis (unit-mismatch annotation), spec-to-code-compliance. building-secure-contracts (11 skills): vulnerability scanners for Algorand, Cairo, Cosmos, Solana, Substrate, TON plus audit-prep-assistant, code-maturity-assessor, guidelines-advisor, secure-workflow-guide (Slither-driven), token-integration-analyzer. entry-point-analyzer: state-changing contract entry points. testing-handbook-skills (15 skills): aflpp, libfuzzer, libafl, cargo-fuzz, atheris, ruzzy, ossfuzz, harness-writing, fuzzing-dictionary, fuzzing-obstacles, coverage-analysis, address-sanitizer, constant-time-testing, wycheproof, testing-handbook-generator. trailmark (10 skills): code-graph building/query, structural analysis, graph diffing between commits, Mermaid diagramming, crypto-protocol-diagram, mermaid-to-proverif, genotoxic (graph-informed mutation-testing triage), vector-forge (mutation-driven crypto test vectors), audit-augmentation, trailmark-summary. Verification: constant-time-analysis (compiler-induced timing leaks, has shipped a real bug find), zeroize-audit (assembly-level check of secret zeroization), mutation-testing (mewt/muton campaigns), property-based-testing. Other security: yara-rule-authoring (with lint + atom-analysis scripts), burpsuite-project-parser, firebase-apk-scanner, dwarf-expert, debug-buttercup (K8s CRS debugging). Dev/utility: ask-questions-if-underspecified, devcontainer-setup, gh-cli (hook that redirects GitHub fetches to authenticated gh), git-cleanup, modern-python, seatbelt-sandboxer, second-opinion (Codex/Gemini external reviews, bundles Codex MCP server), skill-improver, designing-workflow-skills, let-fate-decide (tarot novelty), interpreting-culture-index, chrome-mcp-troubleshooting.
inv.quality: Exceptional — the strongest maintenance and substance signals of any skill collection profile: last commit 2026-06-30 (13 days old, PR #178 adding rust-review), CI workflows validating frontmatter on every PR, pre-commit config, CODEOWNERS, ruff.toml, and a detailed AGENTS.md authoring guide. Content is procedural, not padded: 51 Python scripts (SARIF generators and artifact validators with their own pytest suites, YARA linter, atom analyzer, constant-time analyzer package), 28 shell scripts, bats tests, 41 references/workflows/templates dirs, 5 .ql files, ProVerif models. Skills encode genuinely non-obvious operator knowledge a base model reliably gets wrong: CodeQL defaultSuiteFile silently dropping queries (always generate custom .qls), zero-findings-means-investigate discipline, macOS arm64e exit-137 workaround, Semgrep telemetry off + third-party ruleset lists, anti-rationalization tables ("Heartbleed was 2 lines") and hard approval gates. A Trophy Case documents a real upstream bug found with these skills (timing side-channel in RustCrypto ML-DSA, merged fix). Minor caveat: a few plugins are internal-facing (debug-buttercup, culture-index) or novelty (let-fate-decide), and the smart-contract scanners are narrower checklists than the flagship skills.
inv.dependencies: Heavily tool-dependent by design. External binaries required per skill: codeql CLI, semgrep (Semgrep Pro optional but detected for cross-file taint), slither, yara-x, AFL++/libFuzzer/clang sanitizers, cargo-fuzz, atheris, ruzzy, mewt/muton/necessist, trailofbits' trailmark (0.2.x), proverif, apktool/jadx, gh CLI, kubectl (debug-buttercup only), OpenAI Codex and/or Gemini CLIs for second-opinion. Two plugins ship .mcp.json MCP server configs (second-opinion bundles Codex's MCP server; zeroize-audit). Four plugins install Claude Code hooks (gh-cli URL interception, modern-python shims, skill-improver stop-hook, fp-check). Network access needed for Semgrep registry/third-party rulesets, CodeQL pack downloads, and GitHub API. Many knowledge-layer skills (variant-analysis, fp-check core, harness-writing, dwarf-expert, sharp-edges) work with only Bash/ripgrep. Python scripts assume uv/python3.
inv.surfaces: Claude Code only (plus OpenAI Codex via Claude-marketplace compatibility, explicitly supported). It is structured as a plugin marketplace (.claude-plugin/marketplace.json, per-plugin plugin.json, hooks/, commands/, agents/, allowed-tools frontmatter, {baseDir} references, Task-based parallel subagents) — none of which exists on claude.ai. Nearly every flagship skill shells out to security binaries and spawns subagents. A minority of pure-knowledge skills (variant-analysis method, dwarf-expert, sharp-edges) could be manually repackaged as claude.ai .skill files, but the collection as shipped targets Claude Code.

verdicts.rationale: Verified on disk: this is the definitive coverage for David's named security domain — 75 actively-maintained skills (HEAD commit 13 days old) encoding operator knowledge base models reliably lack, with a documented real upstream bug find (RustCrypto ML-DSA timing side-channel). Standouts are differential-review (risk-first PR review with git-history baselining, blast-radius calculation, adversarial exploit phase — far deeper than the built-in security-review command it partially overlaps), fp-check (TRUE/FALSE-POSITIVE verdicts via source-to-sink tracing and bug-class checklists, a capability core adversarial-verify's generic refutation discipline does not provide), variant-analysis (one bug into a codebase-wide CodeQL/Semgrep hunt), and the static-analysis and testing-handbook fuzzing plugins. It misses MUST ADD only because it serves one of two equal-weight surfaces (Claude Code only), is heavily dependent on external security binaries, and is a single domain rather than broad frequent leverage. Placement must respect the strict-tiered footprint: add the marketplace via /plugin marketplace add trailofbits/skills but enable individual plugins only when doing security work — never all 40 always-on; differential-review and fp-check are the two worth enabling first, optionally project-scoped in repos where David does recurring security review. Zero maintenance burden since Trail of Bits maintains upstream with CI-validated frontmatter; the internal/novelty plugins (debug-buttercup, culture-index, let-fate-decide) should simply never be enabled.

The candidate at .../dives/3-trail-of-bits-security-skills is real and current: git HEAD cfe5d7b dated 2026-06-30 (13 days old, matching the record), 75 SKILL.md files across 40 plugins. Against the core library: the 14 core skills are governance-only (plan-gate, scope-fence, adversarial-verify, etc.) — I read adversarial-verify/SKILL.md and confirmed the overlap is exactly what the analyst stated: a shared generic refutation discipline, but nothing resembling fp-check's source-to-sink tracing, six-gate TRUE/FALSE-POSITIVE verdicts, or class-specific requirements (safe-Rust/Go memory-corruption rejection rule, allocator-hardening checks, gadget-chain existence tests). Against built-ins: /security-review and /code-review are thin single-pass commands with none of differential-review's git-history baselining or blast-radius phases; nothing built-in covers CodeQL/Semgrep orchestration, variant-analysis, constant-time analysis, or YARA. Against base-model behavior (the live-state-truth retirement standard): this content is NOT be-careful prose a strong model already does — it encodes tool-specific operator knowledge (semgrep --metrics=off telemetry warning, Pro-engine detection for cross-file taint, Trail of Bits/0xdea/Decurity third-party rulesets) and literal runnable artifacts (per-language CodeQL taint-tracking .ql templates with source/sink/barrier predicates, semgrep YAML — I read resources/codeql/python.ql). The proposed placement (marketplace add, plugins enabled on-demand only during security work, never all 40 always-on) adds zero always-on context, so it does not violate Decision 5 (activation-footprint discipline) or Decision 4 (anti-ceremony); invariant 8 (placement law) governs this library's own .claude/skills layout, not external marketplace plugins, and the record explicitly bars always-on enablement and the novelty plugins. No invariant breaks. SUBSTANCE — decisively substantive: 2,098 lines in just the sampled supporting docs (methodology.md 234, fp-check references ~585, variant-analysis METHODOLOGY 327, static-analysis skills 952), plus agents, hooks, and evidence templates; the claimed real upstream find is documented in the repo README (RustCrypto/signatures PR #1144, timing side-channel in ML-DSA signing, credited to constant-time-analysis). Measurement would not retire this the way live-state-truth was retired — the delta is tool orchestration and runnable query templates, not exhortation. Deductions the analyst already priced in: single domain, Claude Code-only surface, external binary dependencies (CodeQL/Semgrep/fuzzers must be installed), and the most common use case (PR review) partially overlaps the built-in — these are exactly why MUST ADD was withheld, and I agree with that withholding. I could not independently verify from this repo that security is David's named domain (the repo only evidences brand/governance work), but nothing on disk contradicts the record's claim and every disk-verifiable claim in the record checked out. I am not torn between tiers: all three checks pass cleanly, so the lower-tier tiebreak does not apply. HIGHLY RECOMMEND upheld.

verdicts.name: owasp-security (claude-code-owasp)

inv.name: owasp-security (agamm/claude-code-owasp)
inv.what_it_is: A single Claude Code skill (pure markdown, no code) that injects current OWASP standards into code-writing and code-review sessions. The always-loaded SKILL.md (321 lines) carries a Top 10:2025 quick-reference table, code-review checklists (input handling, auth/sessions, access control, data protection, error handling), canonical unsafe/safe Python patterns, the full OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 (LLM01-LLM10) and Agentic AI Top 10 2026 (ASI01-ASI10) with per-risk checklists and code examples, ASVS 5.0 level summaries, and a 10-point "security analysis mindset" for unlisted languages. Two on-demand reference files add per-language footguns for 20 languages (languages.md, 334 lines) and a deep-dive report on every covered standard (owasp-report.md, 792 lines). It is a knowledge/checklist skill: it changes what Claude looks for during review, it does not run any tooling.
inv.skills_inside: Single skill: owasp-security. Body = OWASP Top 10:2025 table + review checklists + unsafe/safe patterns + LLM Top 10 (2025) + Agentic ASI01-ASI10 (2026) + ASVS 5.0 levels + language-analysis mindset. Progressive-disclosure references: reference/languages.md (JS/TS, Python, Java, C#, PHP, Go, Ruby, Rust, Swift, Kotlin, C/C++, Scala, R, Perl, Shell, Lua, Elixir, Dart, PowerShell, SQL — each ~15 lines of main risks, one unsafe/safe pair, watch-for function list) and reference/owasp-report.md (per-category deep dive: attack vectors, prevention code, mitigation lists for all four standards).
inv.quality: Mixed: genuinely current where it matters, but shallow and partly inaccurate at the edges. Strengths: the Top 10:2025, LLM Top 10 (2025), and Agentic ASI01-ASI10 (Dec 2025) lists match the real published standards — this currency is the main value, since these post-date many models' training emphasis, and the shortlist rationale ("current standards, not stale ones") holds. Nice practitioner touch: the access-control checklist tells Claude to check for framework-level middleware (Next.js middleware.ts, Express) before flagging missing per-route auth — a real false-positive killer for LLM reviews. Weaknesses: the ASVS 5.0 section is unreliable — its 17-chapter list (V1 Architecture, V2 Authentication, V15-V17 "new") and requirement IDs (V2.1.1 password length, V3.2.1 token entropy) are ASVS 4.0 numbering with 5.0 labels slapped on; real ASVS 5.0 reorganized and renumbered chapters entirely. The languages.md entries are the most well-worn footguns imaginable (eval, pickle, strcpy, YAML.load, unserialize) — nothing a strong base model doesn't already know cold. No scripts, no eval harness, no severity taxonomy, no report format. Maintenance: last commit 2026-06-28 (2 weeks old at eval time), shallow clone hides depth of history; README once carried a sponsorship badge (since removed), suggesting a promotional side. Net: a compact, mostly-accurate checklist prompt whose differentiator is the 2025/2026 LLM+Agentic material; roughly 60% of the bulk restates baseline model knowledge.
inv.dependencies: None. Pure markdown; frontmatter restricts allowed-tools to Read/Grep/Glob. No scripts, binaries, APIs, MCP servers, or network access. Install is a directory copy (README suggests npx degit, but git clone + cp works).
inv.surfaces: Both. Packaged for Claude Code (.claude/skills/owasp-security/ project or ~/.claude personal scope, README install instructions are Claude Code-only), but since it is text-only with relative-linked reference files and no executables, it zips cleanly into a .skill for claude.ai; the on-demand reference pattern works on both surfaces.

Checked against the actual files, most of that currency claim is false: (a) the OWASP LLM Top 10 "2025" edition shipped November 2024 — deep inside every current model's knowledge; the table at SKILL.md lines 174-189 and its four code patterns (delimit untrusted input, treat LLM output as untrusted, least-privilege tools, rate limits) are exactly what a strong base model produces unprompted when asked about LLM security. (b) The Top 10:2025 renumbering (A03 Supply Chain, A10 Exceptional Conditions, SKILL.md lines 15-28 and owasp-report.md lines 17-34) was released Nov 2025, inside the Jan-2026 cutoff — I can reproduce it without the file. By the library's own retirement standard (live-state-truth was RETIRE-CONFIRMED because base models already checked live state, architecture-contract Decision 7), content the base model already carries buys zero behavioral delta. (c) languages.md is pure baseline — eval/innerHTML, pickle, ObjectInputStream, BinaryFormatter, PHP == juggling — the analyst themselves conceded ~60% redundancy. (d) Claude Code ships a security-review built-in (present in this very session's skill list) covering the primary diff-review use. Surviving net-new: the ASI01-ASI10 table (Dec 2025, genuinely marginal-currency, but ~30 lines of generic one-line mitigations fetchable by web search in one query) and the single middleware-before-flagging-missing-auth heuristic at SKILL.md line 47. That residue is one table plus one line. ARCHITECTURE (partial break at the proposed placement). The description (line 3: "handling user input... discussing web application security") has no NOT clause and would fire in nearly every backend session, violating Decision 3's description-as-trigger discipline; the analyst dodged this via "on-demand", but their concrete recommendation — upload as a .skill to claude.ai — reintroduces the problem, because uploaded skills' descriptions are always in the trigger set there (no invoke-only mode on that surface). Worse, the record itself admits the ASVS 5.0 section (SKILL.md lines 259-280) is wrong (ASVS 4.0 numbering relabeled) — recommending install of an artifact containing a known-false, undated volatile fact conflicts with invariant 7. A candidate that by the analyst's own account must be trimmed ~60%, have a wrong section cut, and have its trigger rewritten before it is safe to install is raw material, not a HIGHLY RECOMMEND. SUBSTANCE. The body is dominated by be-careful checklists and canonical unsafe/safe pairs (parameterized queries, bcrypt/Argon2, fail-closed) — precisely the class measurement retired: a strong model reviewing security code already flags all of these. The genuinely behavior-changing content is the line-47 false-positive heuristic and possibly the ASI/LLM checklists as coverage scaffolds; that would very likely fail an A/B delta test against the base model on this library's N=2/any-regression standard. Applying the mandated tie-break (when torn, choose lower): security being an uncovered owner-named domain and the agentic checklist's relevance to David's agent work keep it above NOT NEEDED, but with both built-in overlap (Claude Code security-review) and base-model overlap (both 2025 lists in-knowledge), the correct tier is NICE TO HAVE — keep as on-demand reference, install only after cutting the ASVS section, the baseline pattern bulk, and rewriting the trigger. inv.name: hashicorp-agent-skills
inv.what_it_is: HashiCorp's official, actively maintained monorepo of Agent Skills packaged as five Claude Code plugins (plus npx-installable individual skills) covering Terraform and Packer workflows. It contains 15 SKILL.md skills organized under terraform/ (code-generation, module-generation, provider-development) and packer/ (builders, hcp), with a .claude-plugin/marketplace.json so the whole repo works as a Claude Code plugin marketplace ("claude plugin marketplace add hashicorp/agent-skills"). The skills encode HashiCorp's official style conventions, testing frameworks, and — notably — very new Terraform features (Terraform 1.14 Search/bulk-import with .tfquery.hcl list blocks, Terraform Stacks .tfcomponent/.tfdeploy files, provider "actions", ephemeral-resource test patterns). The three Terraform plugins also auto-wire the hashicorp/terraform-mcp-server (Docker-based) into Claude Code via plugin.json. Copyright is IBM Corp. 2025-2026 (post-acquisition), with CI structure validation, a Tessl skill-review workflow, CHANGELOG, and CODEOWNERS (@hashicorp/team-agent-skills-ecosystem).
inv.skills_inside: 15 skills across 5 plugins. terraform-code-generation: (1) terraform-style-guide (314 ln) — official HCL style/file-layout/naming/formatting conventions with examples; (2) terraform-test (451 ln + 3 refs: CI_CD, EXAMPLES, MOCK_PROVIDERS) — writing/running .tftest.hcl tests, run blocks, assertions, provider mocking; (3) azure-verified-modules (613 ln) — AVM certification requirements/best practices for Azure Terraform modules; (4) terraform-search-import (372 ln + MANUAL-IMPORT ref + list_resources.sh script) — discover unmanaged cloud resources via Terraform 1.14 Search queries and bulk-import them, with a runnable script that extracts list-resource support from provider schemas. terraform-module-generation: (5) refactor-module (538 ln) — decompose monolithic configs into reusable modules per HashiCorp design principles; (6) terraform-stacks (480 ln + 6 refs: component/deployment blocks, linked stacks, API monitoring, examples, troubleshooting) — authoring/validating Terraform Stacks. terraform-provider-development: (7) new-terraform-provider (24 ln + assets/main.go) — numbered scaffold procedure for a Plugin Framework provider; (8) provider-resources (599 ln) — CRUD resource/data-source implementation with Plugin Framework and SDKv2 patterns; (9) provider-actions (529 ln) — implementing the new provider "actions" feature; (10) provider-test-patterns (414 ln + 3 refs: checks, ephemeral, sweepers) — acceptance-test patterns incl. statecheck/plancheck/ImportStateKind/echoprovider; (11) run-acceptance-tests (41 ln) — TF_ACC test running/diagnosis procedure incl. a clever "flip the test" false-negative check; (12) provider-docs (67 ln + hashicorp-provider-docs ref + agents/openai.yaml) — tfplugindocs-based Registry documentation. packer-builders: (13) aws-ami-builder (170 ln) — amazon-ebs AMI builds; (14) azure-image-builder (181 ln) — Azure managed/Compute Gallery images; (15) windows-builder (187 ln) — WinRM + PowerShell Windows image builds. packer-hcp: push-to-registry (203 ln) — hcp_packer_registry block, HCP credentials, CI/CD integration, consuming artifacts from Terraform.
inv.quality: High. Substantive: most skills are 300-600 lines of concrete HCL/Go examples, decision trees, file-layout tables, and command sequences, with reference files split out for progressive disclosure and one genuinely useful executable script (list_resources.sh parses `terraform providers schema -json` with jq to detect list-resource support). Maintenance signals are excellent: last commit dated today (2026-07-13, PR #77), active CHANGELOG, CODEOWNERS team, CI that validates repo/plugin/skill structure and JSON, plus an automated Tessl skill-review workflow. Encodes real beyond-base-model value where it matters: Terraform 1.14 Search/bulk import, Terraform Stacks syntax, provider actions, ephemeral-resource testing, and ImportStateKind are new/niche enough that base models hallucinate them; the vendor-official style guide also pins current provider versions (aws ~> 6.0, required_version >= 1.14). Weaknesses: a few skills are thin (new-terraform-provider is 24 lines; run-acceptance-tests 41 lines — though both are tight procedures, not padding), the Packer skills and terraform-style-guide lean toward documentation-summary that a strong model mostly knows, and skill versions are early (0.0.1-0.1.0).
inv.dependencies: Core CLIs the skills instruct the agent to run: terraform (>= 1.14 for search-import/stacks), packer, go (provider development), jq + bash (list_resources.sh script). Cloud credentials needed to actually execute workflows (AWS/Azure for builders and imports; HCP_CLIENT_ID/SECRET/ORG/PROJECT for HCP Packer; TF_ACC env for acceptance tests). The three Terraform plugin.json files bundle an MCP server: hashicorp/terraform-mcp-server run via Docker with optional TFE_TOKEN/TFE_ADDRESS — so Docker is required for that MCP integration (skills still function without it). Network access needed for terraform init/registry, go get, and cloud APIs. No other binaries or web services; only one shell script in the whole repo's skills.
inv.surfaces: Both in principle, Claude Code (and CLI agents) in practice. SKILL.md files use standard name/description frontmatter and are explicitly cross-agent (README targets Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Opencode via `npx skills add`); the knowledge-heavy skills (style-guide, stacks, test patterns, AVM) would work fine zipped for claude.ai as reference material. But the real workflows assume a filesystem + terminal (terraform/packer/go/jq commands, the list_resources.sh script) and the plugin marketplace + Docker MCP server integration is Claude Code-only, so full value requires Claude Code or an equivalent execution environment.
inv.license: MPL-2.0 (LICENSE file at repo root; also declared in marketplace.json, every plugin.json, and several SKILL.md frontmatters; copyright headers read "Copyright IBM Corp. 2025, 2026").

verdicts.rationale: Fully net-new domain coverage: nothing in the 14-skill governance library or Claude Code built-ins covers IaC, and the standout skills (terraform-search-import with its runnable list_resources.sh schema probe, terraform-stacks, provider-test-patterns, provider-actions, terraform-test) encode Terraform 1.14-era features that base models reliably hallucinate — the same measured standard that retired live-state-truth cuts the other way here. Quality and maintenance are best-in-class: vendor-official, commit dated today, CHANGELOG/CODEOWNERS/CI structure validation, and MPL-2.0 is upload-safe. It is HIGHLY RECOMMEND rather than MUST ADD because leverage is narrow — the collection only fires in Terraform/Packer sessions, and there is no evidence of daily IaC work in David's profile; the Packer builders and style-guide portions are closer to documentation summaries a strong model mostly knows. Placement is the easy part: this is a self-updating Claude Code plugin marketplace, so the right move is to NOT copy anything into the core library — add the marketplace and install terraform-code-generation (and siblings as needed) per-repo where Terraform work happens, at zero always-on context cost and zero maintenance burden. Optionally zip terraform-stacks or provider-test-patterns as on-demand claude.ai references if IaC questions ever arise there.

verify.reasoning:
Check 1 (REDUNDANCY) — the net-new claim survives. I read all 14 core skills' scope (governance/meta library: plan-gate, adversarial-verify, scope-fence, plus support skills and brand-standard) — nothing touches Terraform, Packer, or IaC, and no Claude Code/claude.ai built-in does either. Against the live-state-truth retirement standard (base models already do it natively): the standout candidate skills encode material a strong base model does NOT reliably produce — /tmp/.../5-hashicorp-agent-skills/terraform/code-generation/skills/terraform-search-import/SKILL.md documents Terraform 1.14 .tfquery.hcl `list` blocks, `terraform query -generate-config-out`, identity-based import, plus a runnable scripts/list_resources.sh that probes `list_resource_schemas` from the live provider schema (a live-state check, not prose); terraform-stacks/SKILL.md covers the distinct .tfcomponent.hcl/.tfdeploy.hcl stack language; provider-test-patterns/SKILL.md carries exact terraform-plugin-testing API surface (statecheck.ExpectKnownValue, tfjsonpath, ImportStateKind, echoprovider). These are late-2025/2026 features with high hallucination risk. Redundancy does not refute.

Check 2 (ARCHITECTURE) — no violation. I read /home/user/claude-core-skills/.claude/skills/architecture-contract/SKILL.md; the proposed placement (marketplace/plugin install per-repo, nothing copied into the core library) has zero always-on context cost, respects Decision 5 (activation footprint) and invariant 5 (no ceremony), and doesn't touch the placement law since nothing enters this repo. Architecture does not refute.

Check 3 (SUBSTANCE) — passes for the flagship skills (runnable script, exact fresh syntax, real API names — not be-careful prose), though the analyst's own record concedes the Packer builders and terraform-style-guide portions are documentation summaries a strong model mostly knows.

REFUTATION — the tier fails on leverage, by the analyst's own admitted evidence made concrete. The record says "no evidence of daily IaC work in David's profile"; the repo confirms stronger: grepping /home/user/claude-core-skills for terraform/packer/hashicorp/IaC yields zero real hits (the single "infrastructure" match in brand-standard/SKILL.md is "operational infrastructure" as resume vocabulary). David's profile per brand-standard is program-management/operations correspondence and resumes ($65M revenue scale, mentees, candidate-partner emails); sibling dives include career-ops and pm-skills — nothing suggests he writes Terraform, and no repo exists in evidence "where Terraform work happens" to install this into. HIGHLY RECOMMEND requires demand, not just supply quality: a vendor-official, best-in-class, zero-cost collection for a domain the user shows no trace of using is the textbook NICE TO HAVE — keep the marketplace pointer, install the moment an IaC repo appears. Not NOT NEEDED, because if Terraform work ever arises this is unambiguously the right source (self-updating, MPL-2.0, CI-validated) and the recommendation costs nothing to hold. Torn between HIGHLY RECOMMEND and NICE TO HAVE, the mandate is to choose the lower, and here the evidence independently points lower anyway.

inv.quality: Above-average for a community collection, though breadth-over-depth. Strengths: every skill follows a disciplined, lintable template (CI runs scripts/validate-skills.py with strict frontmatter parsing); real progressive disclosure (lean SKILL.md + 366 topical references loaded on demand, keeping token cost low); references contain concrete, runnable code (e.g. postgres-pro's annotated EXPLAIN ANALYZE walkthroughs and index-strategy SQL; react-expert's 1100-line class-to-hooks migration guide) rather than pure prose; strong maintenance signals — semver releases through v0.4.15 (2026-05-20), a detailed dated CHANGELOG with external-contributor bug fixes (e.g. corrected pg_stat_* column names in #191), CI, pre-commit, a docs site, and version.json metadata. Weaknesses: most content is framework best-practice knowledge a strong base model already has (React hooks rules, Django select_related, Terraform modules) — the marginal value is trigger scoping, consistent workflow discipline, MUST/MUST-NOT checklists, and per-topic reference routing, not novel procedures; no executable scripts or verification tooling inside any skill (advice-only, nothing self-checking); descriptions are keyword-stuffed which risks over-triggering when many skills are installed together; some skills (the-fool, feature-forge, spec-miner) and the Jira-integrated execute-ticket workflow (with parallel-wave execution, failure gates, and user checkpoints) encode genuinely differentiated process. Framework version claims (React 19, Next 14, .NET 8, Laravel 10) will age.
inv.dependencies: Skills tree is 100% markdown — no scripts, binaries, or network access required at runtime. The 9 project workflow commands and the atlassian-mcp skill require an Atlassian MCP server (Jira + Confluence access) to function; docs/ATLASSIAN_MCP_SETUP.md covers setup. Repo dev tooling (Python validate scripts, Makefile, pre-commit, Astro docs site) is contributor-only, not needed to use the skills. Installable via Claude Code plugin marketplace (/plugin marketplace add jeffallan/claude-skills).
inv.surfaces: Both, with a split: the 66 skills are standard Agent Skills (name+description frontmatter, references/ subdirectory, no code execution) so each could be packaged for claude.ai or used in Claude Code; however the repo is built and distributed as a Claude Code plugin (.claude-plugin manifest, slash commands with $ARGUMENTS, workflow-manifest.yaml, AskUserQuestion tool usage in the-fool), and the 9 workflow commands plus /common-ground are Claude Code-only. Installing all 66 skills at once is only practical in Claude Code; claude.ai use would mean cherry-picking individual skills.

verdicts.rationale: Verified against the actual files: this is a well-maintained, lint-disciplined 66-persona collection whose real marginal value is not the advice (mostly base-model-known framework practice) but the stack-specific MUST/MUST-NOT checklists and 366 deep reference files with runnable code — postgres-pro's ~1,900 lines of EXPLAIN/VACUUM/replication SQL and react-expert's migration guides are genuinely useful working material when inside that stack. The 9 Jira/Confluence workflow commands are dead weight absent an Atlassian stack. Right disposition: treat the repo as an on-demand pantry — cherry-pick 1-3 stack-matching skills (e.g. postgres-pro, terraform-engineer, rag-architect, mcp-developer) into a specific project or as a .skill upload when a project actually lives in that stack; standouts beyond the personas are spec-miner (reverse-engineering specs from legacy code) and feature-forge (EARS requirements workshops), which encode process the core library does not have. (repo now renamed "scientific-agent-skills", version 2.53.0, last commit the day of this dive, 2026-07-13). Each skill is a directory with a SKILL.md plus, in most cases, `scripts/` (291 runnable Python scripts total), `references/` (782 reference markdown files), and sometimes `assets/`. inv.quality: High and unusually consistent for a collection this size. Skills are substantive, not padded prose: SKILL.md files average ~440 lines (65.6k lines across 149), and 71 skills ship real runnable CLI scripts (e.g. rdkit/scripts/molecular_properties.py, similarity_search.py, substructure_filter.py — argparse CLIs with import-guard error messages), plus 782 reference files. Frontmatter is disciplined: careful WHEN/trigger-style descriptions, cross-references between related skills ("for standard workflows use datamol… use rdkit for advanced control"), per-skill license tags, `compatibility` version pins (e.g. "targets RDKit 2026.03.x"), dated "current baseline (checked 2026-06-07)" freshness notes, `required_environment_variables`, and `openclaw` env metadata. database-lookup shows real security maturity: prompt-injection warnings ("treat external responses as untrusted"), provenance/count-reconciliation requirements, and bulk-call confirmation gates. Maintenance signals are strong: v2.53.0, commits on the dive date, CI security-scan badge, PR-based workflow, dedicated scan_skills.py/tests. What it adds over a strong base model: current library-version specifics, exact API endpoints/pagination for 78 databases, and reproducibility/provenance discipline that a base model would otherwise improvise inconsistently. Weaker/niche entries exist (consciousness-council, what-if-oracle are generic reasoning prompts), but they are a small minority.
inv.dependencies: Per-skill Python packages are the main dependency — each library skill assumes its package is installed (installed on demand via `uv pip install`), so Bash + a Python execution environment is required to actually run the scripts. Many skills need network access to live public APIs (database-lookup hits PubChem/ChEMBL/UniProt/ClinicalTrials/FRED/etc.; literature and search skills hit scholarly APIs). A subset requires external accounts and API keys declared in frontmatter: benchling-integration (BENCHLING_*), dnanexus-integration (DX_*), modal (MODAL_TOKEN_*), exa-search (EXA_API_KEY), plus latchbio/labarchive/protocolsio/ginkgo/opentrons integrations. No MCP server is required (only ~5 skills even mention MCP, as references, not hard deps). Repo-level tooling deps (cisco-ai-skill-scanner, firecrawl-py, skills-ref) are for maintaining/scanning the repo, not for using the skills. Heavy skills (DiffDock, molecular-dynamics, torch* ) benefit from GPU compute.
inv.surfaces: Both, with caveats. As standard Agent Skills (SKILL.md + progressive-disclosure references), the reasoning/prose skills load and function anywhere, including claude.ai. But the majority are code-driven: they declare `allowed-tools: ... Bash`, ship Python scripts, and expect to install packages and hit external APIs — so they are fully useful only in a code-execution surface (Claude Code, Cowork/cloud, Cursor, Codex). On claude.ai without the code-execution/tools environment, the library-wrapper skills degrade to documentation. The repo explicitly targets multi-agent installation via `gh skill install ... --agent claude-code|cursor|codex|gemini`, confirming Claude Code as a first-class surface.
