verdicts.name: tapestry-skills-for-claude-code (michalparkola: tapestry, article-extractor, youtube-transcript, ship-learn-next)

inv.name: tapestry-skills-for-claude-code
inv.what_it_is: A 7-skill personal-productivity collection by michalparkola built around a "capture learning content, then force yourself to act on it" pipeline. The core flow is: learn-this (orchestrator, formerly branded "tapestry" — its body is still titled "Tapestry: Unified Content Extraction + Action Planning") detects whether a URL is YouTube/article/PDF, extracts clean text via the youtube-transcript or article-extractor skills, then auto-generates a "Ship-Learn-Next" 5-rep action plan via the ship-learn-next skill and asks "When will you ship Rep 1?". Around that pipeline sit three standalone prompt-persona skills: scrum-sage (agile-coach persona), session-log (append session summaries to a weekly ISO-week markdown journal), and unblock-action (turn a vague stuck task into a concrete next action). Note the shortlist blurb oversells one aspect: there is no document-interlinking/knowledge-network skill anywhere in the repo — it is extract-and-plan, not a Zettelkasten/synthesis system.
inv.skills_inside: 7 skills, each a single SKILL.md (no scripts/ or references/ dirs): (1) learn-this — master orchestrator: URL type detection (YouTube/PDF/article) + inline bash for each extraction path + auto-invokes ship-learn-next; duplicates code from skills 2-3. (2) youtube-transcript — yt-dlp subtitle download with manual-subs → auto-subs → Whisper-transcription fallback chain, plus an inline Python VTT deduplicator (handles YouTube's overlapping-caption duplicate-line quirk) and title-based filenames. (3) article-extractor — clean article text via reader-cli (Mozilla Readability) or trafilatura, with a dependency-free curl+HTMLParser fallback. (4) ship-learn-next — prompt-only framework that converts a transcript/article into a 5-rep progressive action plan markdown file ("100 reps beats 100 hours of study"). (5) scrum-sage — Scrum Master / agility coach persona (Sutherland/Ohno/"First Principles"), sprint analysis modes, anti-pattern detection; prompt-only. (6) session-log — summarize the conversation into a `YYYY-wWW Agent Log.md` weekly file with strict append/format rules and CHUNK markers. (7) unblock-action — 2-3-question Socratic flow producing an "action card" (deliverable, today-scope, first physical action). The shortlisted "tapestry" name does not exist as a directory; learn-this is that skill renamed.
inv.quality: Mixed but above-average for the genre. The two extraction skills encode genuinely useful operational know-how a base model gets wrong or forgets: the VTT duplicate-line dedup for YouTube auto-captions, the manual-subs-before-auto-subs-before-Whisper fallback order with explicit user-confirmation gates before large downloads, and the reader→trafilatura→bare-HTMLParser cascade. Weaknesses: all code lives as inline bash/python blocks in SKILL.md (nothing is an executable script, so Claude re-types everything); learn-this copy-pastes the extraction code from the other two skills (drift risk); several workflows use interactive `read -r` prompts that do not work in Claude Code's non-interactive Bash; scrum-sage is padded persona prose with pseudo-scientific framing ("Free Energy Principle", "entropy analysis"); ship-learn-next/unblock-action are decent but are things a strong model can improvise — their value is consistency of format, not capability. Maintenance: 12 commits total isn't verifiable from a shallow clone, but last commit 2026-03-11 ("fix username"), ~4 months old; README still points install instructions at the repo's old name (tapestry-skills), a small hygiene lapse. Frontmatter is valid (name/description/allowed-tools) with reasonable trigger descriptions.
inv.dependencies: External binaries: yt-dlp (auto-installs via brew/apt/pip3), python3 (inline dedup/JSON scripts), curl; optional: openai-whisper (pip, 1-10GB models) for no-subtitle videos, reader-cli (npm) or trafilatura (pip) for article extraction (has a stdlib fallback), pdftotext from poppler for PDFs. Network access required to YouTube and arbitrary article URLs (yt-dlp from datacenter IPs is frequently bot-blocked by YouTube — a real risk in sandboxed environments). Some install paths use `sudo apt`, which may prompt. No MCP servers, no API keys, no paid services. ship-learn-next, scrum-sage, session-log, unblock-action need only Read/Write (and session-log needs `date`).
inv.surfaces: Claude Code primarily (repo is explicitly packaged for ~/.claude/skills with an install.sh, and uses allowed-tools frontmatter). The three extraction-pipeline skills (learn-this, youtube-transcript, article-extractor) are Claude Code-only in practice: they need Bash, binary installation, and unrestricted network. The four prompt-only skills (ship-learn-next, unblock-action, scrum-sage, session-log) are surface-portable and could be repackaged as claude.ai skills, though session-log assumes file append to a persistent working directory.
inv.license: MIT (LICENSE file, copyright 2025 "Tapestry Skills Contributors"; README confirms).

verdicts.overlaps_with: Claude Code WebFetch + claude.ai native web fetch/search (covers article-extractor); base-model improvisation per the live-state-truth retirement standard (covers ship-learn-next, unblock-action, scrum-sage, session-log); no core-library skill overlaps — lessons-ledger was checked against session-log and is a different artifact (mistakes ledger with evidence discipline, not a session-summary journal)
verdicts.surfaces: Claude Code only for the standout (youtube-transcript needs Bash, yt-dlp install, and network; works best on local machines since YouTube bot-blocks datacenter IPs, limiting cloud/sandbox sessions). The four prompt-only sub-skills are technically portable to claude.ai but not worth the upload.
verdicts.license: MIT (LICENSE file present, copyright 2025 Tapestry Skills Contributors)
verdicts.rationale: Tiered by its one defensible sub-skill: youtube-transcript is genuinely net-new — no surface can pull YouTube transcripts natively, and it encodes operational knowledge models get wrong (manual-subs → auto-subs → Whisper fallback with confirmation gates before large downloads, and the VTT overlapping-caption dedup script for YouTube's duplicate-line quirk). The other six sub-skills fail David's own retirement standard: article-extractor duplicates WebFetch/claude.ai web fetch, learn-this is a thin dispatcher that copy-pastes extraction code from the other skills (verified drift risk), and ship-learn-next/unblock-action/session-log/scrum-sage are improvisable prompt frameworks whose only value is format consistency. Quality issues verified in the files — interactive `read -r` prompts that break in non-interactive Bash, all code inline rather than as executable scripts, and yt-dlp datacenter-IP bot-blocking — cap it below HIGHLY RECOMMEND. Recommended action: extract youtube-transcript alone as an on-demand Claude Code skill (invoke when a YouTube transcript is actually needed; MIT permits carrying it into the library after a skill-authoring rewrite that scripts the workflow and removes the interactive prompts); skip the other six entirely.

verdicts.name: avoid-ai-writing

inv.name: avoid-ai-writing
inv.what_it_is: A single-file writing-quality skill (683-line SKILL.md, v3.15.0) that audits and rewrites text to remove AI writing patterns ("AI-isms"). It defines three modes (rewrite with a built-in second corrective pass, detect-only, edit-in-place via the Edit tool), a 3-tier vocabulary replacement system (~109 word entries plus 10 boilerplate phrases: Tier 1 always flag, Tier 2 flag in clusters, Tier 3 flag by density), 53 pattern categories spanning structure, rhythm, chatbot artifacts, AI-tool fingerprints (citeturn tokens, utm_source=chatgpt.com), and conversational-register tells, all modulated by a context-profile tolerance matrix (linkedin/blog/technical-blog/investor-email/docs/casual) and 5 optional voice profiles. The repo also ships a zero-dependency Node/browser detector engine (detector/patterns.js, 1,722 lines, 45 typed detectors with homoglyph/zero-width-char normalization and a GPTZero-shaped trinary classifier) that is the deterministic, testable implementation of the regex-detectable subset. Note the shortlist blurb undercounts it: it is 53 categories and a ~109-entry table, not 21/43 — that description matches an older version.
inv.skills_inside: Single skill: avoid-ai-writing. The repo contains it twice (root SKILL.md and plugins/avoid-ai-writing/skills/avoid-ai-writing/SKILL.md as a Claude Code plugin with .claude-plugin/marketplace.json) — verified byte-identical, with a CI sync check preventing drift. Supporting assets, not separate skills: cursor-rules/avoid-ai-writing.mdc (Cursor port of the same rules) and detector/patterns.js (optional executable detection engine with its own test suite). Body summary: mode selection, what-it-isn't caveats with cited false-positive research, the tiered replacement tables, ~40 pattern sections each with tell/fix/carve-out, severity tiers (P0 credibility killers / P1 AI smell / P2 polish), a self-reference escape hatch for quoted examples, context tolerance matrix, voice profiles with composition rules, and per-mode output formats.
inv.quality: High — among the most substantive writing skills I've seen. Signals: (1) empirically grounded, cites Liang et al. 2023, Jabarian & Imas 2025, and arXiv:2506.07001 on detector false positives, and explicitly frames output as "signals, not proof"; (2) real false-positive engineering — carve-outs per rule (named-contrast exemption, fiction/thought-experiment exemption, technical-blog word exceptions), and the changelog documents rules that were REVERTED from the detector when they flagged human prose (wall-of-text structural gate, unconditional "deeply"); (3) executable verification: detector test suite (848-line fixtures + a CATEGORIES.md anti-drift contract test) passes locally, and three CI workflows guard detector tests, pattern-count drift, and plugin-skill sync; (4) very actively maintained — 15 minor releases in the changelog, v3.13-3.15 shipped 2026-07-07/08, five days before today, with community issues (#39, #53) folded in. It encodes plenty a base model doesn't reliably do unprompted: tiered cluster/density thresholds instead of blanket bans, the tolerance matrix, mechanical fingerprints (citation-markup leaks, AI UTM params, unfilled placeholders, homoglyph bypass tricks), and the over-polishing warning. Caveats: SKILL.md is 69KB, a heavy context load when triggered; CLAUDE.md is stale (still says v3.7.2 and "no tests", contradicting the repo); severity/priority juggling across 53 categories relies on model judgment at execution time.
inv.dependencies: None required for the skill itself — pure markdown instructions, no scripts, binaries, APIs, MCP servers, or network access. Edit mode assumes a file Edit tool (agent-side, present in Claude Code). The optional detector engine requires only Node >= 18 (zero npm dependencies; also runs as a plain browser script) and is not needed for the skill to function.
inv.surfaces: Both. The SKILL.md is standard prose-only instruction with agentskills.io-compatible frontmatter (name + trigger-phrase description), so it works packaged for claude.ai as well as in Claude Code (where it also installs as a plugin via the bundled marketplace.json, or cloned into ~/.claude/skills). Only the edit-in-place mode and the optional Node detector are Claude Code-specific; rewrite and detect modes are pure text-in/text-out. The repo itself claims and supports Claude Code, Cowork, OpenClaw, Cursor, and other agentskills.io agents.
inv.license: MIT (LICENSE file, Copyright (c) 2026 Conor Bronsdon; also declared in package.json and SKILL.md frontmatter).

verdicts.overlaps_with: brand-standard (partial: its banned hype-vocabulary hard rule and voice registers already police a handful of AI-ish words in David-authored external artifacts; no overlap on detection, pattern catalog, or third-party text). No platform built-in or base-model coverage — base models are the source of these patterns, not a reliable remover of them, so the live-state-truth retirement standard does not apply here.
verdicts.surfaces: Both equally. rewrite/detect modes are pure text-in/text-out and work on claude.ai via .skill upload; Claude Code gets all three modes (edit-in-place uses the Edit tool) plus the optional zero-dependency Node detector. agentskills.io-compatible frontmatter, no APIs or network access required.
verdicts.license: MIT (LICENSE file verified, Copyright (c) 2026 Conor Bronsdon; also declared in SKILL.md frontmatter and package.json)
verdicts.rationale: This is genuine net-new discipline the base model cannot self-supply: asked to "humanize" text, models do a shallow word-swap pass, while this skill encodes tiered cluster/density thresholds, mechanical AI fingerprints (citeturn leaks, utm_source=chatgpt.com params, unfilled placeholders), a context-tolerance matrix, per-rule false-positive carve-outs, and an explicit over-polishing warning — all empirically grounded (Liang et al. 2023 cited) and guarded by a passing detector test suite. It directly serves David's research/writing domain and every external-facing draft (emails, LinkedIn, resumes), complementing rather than duplicating brand-standard, which governs what David sounds like while this removes machine tells from any text. It lands at HIGHLY RECOMMEND rather than MUST ADD for exactly the tier-definition reasons: the 69KB body is a heavy per-trigger context load on a Sonnet daily driver, the vocabulary-policing slice partially overlaps brand-standard for David's own artifacts, and its trigger is explicit-request-shaped so leverage depends on invocation. Placement: always-on personal is justified because the per-session cost is only the short description line (body loads on trigger), it fires on both surfaces, and writing polish requests are frequent; if measured trigger noise appears, demote to on-demand .skill upload with no loss of capability. Only the single SKILL.md should be installed — the detector engine, cursor-rules, and plugin wrapper are upstream assets, not part of the footprint.

verify.reasoning: Read the full candidate (dives/9-avoid-ai-writing/SKILL.md, 683 lines/69KB) plus brand-standard/SKILL.md and architecture-contract/SKILL.md. Check 3 (SUBSTANCE) passes: this is not be-careful prose — it ships a tiered vocabulary catalog with cluster/density thresholds, regex-level mechanical fingerprints (citeturn/oaicite leaks §374-378, utm_source=chatgpt.com params §379-382, placeholder regexes §370-372), a P0-P2 severity triage, a 6-profile tolerance matrix, and voice profiles with concrete targets (≤14-word sentences, contraction rates). A strong model asked to "humanize" would not systematically apply the fingerprint checks or thresholds. But Checks 1 and 2 erode the tier. REDUNDANCY: the overlap with brand-standard is materially larger than the analyst's "handful of banned words." For David-authored external artifacts — the highest-frequency surface — brand-standard's registers already enforce the de-AI outcome: numbers-replace-adjectives, every claim quantified or cut, short plain paragraphs, specific warmth, banned hype vocabulary (world-class, best-in-class, cutting-edge, dynamic all also appear in the candidate's tables). The candidate's residual net-new value concentrates on third-party text audits and mechanical fingerprints, a use case with zero evidence of frequency for David. And base-model coverage is unmeasured in either direction: by this library's own standard (live-state-truth was retired only AFTER measured zero delta; weak-point 1 says treat unmeasured triggering as "candidate, not fact"; governance-adoption-campaign gates investment on measurement), an unmeasured ~17k-token per-trigger body on a Sonnet daily driver is speculative spend — the famous Tier 1 items (delve, em dashes, "it's not X—it's Y", rule of three) are exactly the meme-level patterns a 2026 base model already cuts on an explicit "de-AI this" request. ARCHITECTURE: two frictions. (a) Decision 3 requires every description to carry a NOT clause; the candidate's has none, so installed as-is it violates the house description contract and has no routing boundary against brand-standard for the ambiguous "clean up my draft" case. (b) Direct rule conflicts at always-on personal placement: the candidate hard-bans em dashes (target zero) and "genuine/genuinely," and converts bullet lists to prose — while brand-standard Register A characterizes David's voice as "plain-spoken and genuine" with emoji allowed, and Register B mandates action-verb bullets for resumes. Decision 2's precedence law resolves project-vs-governor, not personal-vs-personal, so both firing on the same resume/email polish is exactly the weak-point-2 double-load contradiction the contract warns about, with no resolution rule. Decisive: the analyst's own rationale concedes the trigger is explicit-request-shaped and that demotion "to on-demand .skill upload" costs "no loss of capability." A skill that is only reached by explicit invocation, whose primary surface is already governed by an installed skill it partially contradicts, and whose always-on placement is admitted to be droppable at zero capability cost, is useful-when-asked-for, not a gap the library needs filled. Torn between HIGHLY RECOMMEND and NICE TO HAVE, the mandate says take the lower: NICE TO HAVE, as an on-demand upload (single SKILL.md, NOT clause added, with an explicit deference rule to brand-standard for David-authored artifacts), not always-on personal.

verdicts.name: OpenPaw

inv.name: OpenPaw (npm package: pawmode)
inv.what_it_is: A 42-skill bundle (marketed as 38) plus an ~8,300-line TypeScript setup CLI that converts Claude Code into a local personal assistant. `npx pawmode` runs a wizard that copies chosen SKILL.md files into ~/.claude/skills/, installs the third-party CLI each skill wraps (via Homebrew/npm/pip), appends broad `Bash(<tool> *)` allow rules to ~/.claude/settings.json, installs a regex-based PreToolUse safety hook, writes an identity/persona section into ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (SOUL.md personality, PAW MODE greeting), and optionally wires up a Telegram bridge (via the official telegram@claude-plugins-official plugin), a launchd/cron scheduler with per-run and daily cost caps driven by the Claude Agent SDK, a Stop-hook "self-learning" memory distiller, and a local kanban dashboard. The skills themselves are short CLI cheat-sheets: each maps a personal-ops domain (email, calendar, Spotify, Hue lights, Obsidian, package tracking...) to a specific curated CLI tool with concrete commands, flags, config paths, and auth notes. It is explicitly local-machine, macOS-first tooling — no daemon or cloud service of its own.
inv.skills_inside: 42 skill dirs, each a single SKILL.md (no scripts/assets inside skills; all logic is CLI invocation or installer-side TS): c-core — coordinator/persona (name "c"): PAW MODE greeting, session-start checklist, memory conventions, routing table of all skills; c-memory — persistent markdown memory in ~/.claude/memory/ (MEMORY.md/people/preferences/projects/journal) with write rules and optional Obsidian sync; c-ai — query other LLMs via `llm` or `aichat`; c-apps — Mac App Store via `mas`; c-bluetooth — device management via `blucli`; c-briefing — morning briefing template composed from other installed skills, with cron/launchd recipe; c-browser — headless browser automation via agent-browser/playwright-cli; c-calendar — Google Calendar via `gog` or Apple Calendar via icalBuddy; c-clipboard — clipboard copy/paste/transform; c-contacts — macOS Contacts via AppleScript; c-cron — cron + launchctl via lunchy-go; c-display — brightness + safe trash; c-email — Gmail via `gog` or IMAP via `himalaya` (read/send/search/label); c-files — cloud sync via rclone; c-github — PRs/issues/actions via `gh` + `jq`; c-jira — jira-cli issues/sprints; c-lights — Philips Hue via openhue-cli; c-linear — `linear` CLI issues/branches; c-location — Apple Maps/nearby places via goplaces; c-lockin — focus-mode orchestration via generated distraction-blocking shell scripts; c-messaging — iMessage via imsg, WhatsApp via wacli; c-music — Spotify via spogo; c-network — DNS via doggo, HTTP via httpie; c-notes — Apple Notes/Reminders via memo + remindctl; c-notify — macOS notifications via terminal-notifier; c-notion — notion-cli pages/databases; c-obsidian — obsidian-cli vault ops, daily notes, memory sync; c-research — URL/PDF/YouTube summarization via `summarize`; c-schedule — recurring Claude jobs with cost caps (drives `openpaw schedule`); c-screen — screenshots/OCR via peekaboo, webcam via camsnap; c-secrets — 1Password `op` / Bitwarden `bw`; c-slack — slack-cli messages/uploads; c-speakers — Sonos via sonoscli; c-system — macOS control via m-cli; c-tasks — Todoist/Things/Taskwarrior; c-telegram — Telegram bridge usage/setup; c-timer — timers/pomodoro via terminal-notifier; c-tracking — package tracking via ordercli; c-video — yt-dlp + ffmpeg download/convert/clip; c-video-edit — Remotion/Editly programmatic video (longest, 147 lines); c-voice — STT via sag (ElevenLabs), TTS via macOS `say`; c-weather — wttr.in via curl, no API key.
inv.quality: Mixed but genuinely substantive. The per-skill bodies are lean (39–147 lines, median ~55) and are mostly CLI command references — real flags, config paths (~/.config/himalaya/config.toml, 600-perm token files), auth prerequisites, and sensible guardrails (c-secrets: never print passwords, clipboard only; c-display: trash instead of rm). Little filler prose, though c-core's PAW MODE branding/emoji greeting and the briefing format template are things a base model does unprompted. What it encodes that a base model does not reliably have: the curated toolchain itself — many wrapped CLIs are obscure or 2025-era (spogo, peekaboo, camsnap, ordercli, goplaces, sag, blucli, wacli, gog, remindctl, rtk), so exact commands are real added value if those tools are installed; also the cross-skill conventions (memory file layout, Obsidian bidirectional sync, cost-capped scheduling). The heavier engineering value is in the installer, not the skills: atomic settings.json writes, idempotent permission/hook management, Agent-SDK-driven scheduler with per-run/daily cost caps, migration from other assistants. Weaknesses: skills are shallow enough that a strong model with `--help` could reconstruct most; the safety hook is a crude grep blocklist (trivially bypassed, and the installer grants broad `Bash(<tool> *)` wildcards including email send and file deletion tools); several wrapped CLIs are one-maintainer projects whose interfaces may drift; no tests for skill content. Maintenance signals decent: v1.7.0, last commit 2026-05-23 (~7 weeks old), CI workflow present, active single-author development with real changelog-style commits.
inv.dependencies: Heavy. Node >=18 + npx for the installer (deps: @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk, commander, clack, chalk). Each skill requires its third-party CLI installed via Homebrew/npm/pip (~35 binaries total: gh, jq, rclone, yt-dlp, ffmpeg, playwright, himalaya, gog, todoist/task/things-cli, op, bw, httpie, doggo, m-cli, mas, terminal-notifier, obsidian-cli, notion-cli, openhue-cli, sonoscli, spogo, peekaboo, sag, imsg, wacli, icalBuddy, jira-cli, linear, memo, remindctl, ordercli, goplaces, summarize, camsnap, blucli, brightness, macos-trash, lunchy-go, optional rtk). Per-service auth required: Google OAuth (email/calendar), Telegram bot token, Slack/Notion/Jira/Linear tokens, 1Password/Bitwarden vaults, ElevenLabs key, IMAP credentials. Network access for nearly everything. No MCP servers — deliberately all CLI. Strong macOS bias: ~15 skills are macOS-only (AppleScript, launchd, m-cli, mas, iMessage, Apple Notes); Linux gets partial coverage via cron fallbacks.
inv.surfaces: Claude Code only (local desktop, ideally macOS). Every skill assumes Bash access to locally installed binaries, ~/.claude/* file paths, ~/.config/openpaw state, settings.json permission edits, PreToolUse/Stop hooks, and long-running local processes (Telegram bridge, launchd jobs). None of this exists on claude.ai, and sandboxed/cloud Claude Code lacks the local apps (Spotify, Hue bridge on LAN, iMessage DB) the tools talk to.
inv.license: MIT (LICENSE file, Copyright (c) 2026 Dax; also declared in package.json and README)

verdicts.overlaps_with: Platform built-ins and connectors, not the 14 governors: c-schedule/c-cron vs Claude Code loop + Routines/cron tools; c-research vs deep-research; c-github vs gh + GitHub MCP connectors; c-email/c-calendar/c-music vs Gmail/Calendar/Spotify MCP connectors on claude.ai; c-memory/c-core vs Claude Code native memory and CLAUDE.md; c-weather/c-network/c-browser vs base-model capability; c-core's persona/CLAUDE.md injection conflicts with architecture-contract (Decisions 4/5: anti-ceremony, governed install footprint) and its installer bypasses install-and-surfaces' register-then-verify discipline.
verdicts.surfaces: Claude Code on a local desktop only, strongly macOS-biased (~15 of 42 skills are macOS-only: AppleScript, launchd, iMessage, m-cli, mas); requires locally installed third-party CLIs plus per-service auth. Zero value on claude.ai and in sandboxed/cloud Claude Code — half of David's equally-weighted usage.
verdicts.license: MIT (LICENSE file, Copyright (c) 2026 Dax; also declared in package.json and README)
verdicts.rationale: The genuinely net-new slice is the curated toolchain map: which obscure 2025-era CLI to reach for per personal-ops domain (gog/himalaya for email, imsg/wacli for iMessage/WhatsApp, openhue-cli for Hue, sonoscli for Sonos, peekaboo for screenshots/OCR, ordercli for package tracking) with real flags, config paths, and auth notes — smart-home, iMessage, and local-Mac control that no core skill, built-in, or connector covers. Everything else is already served: scheduling by loop/Routines, research by deep-research, GitHub/email/calendar/music by connectors and gh, memory by Claude Code's native memory, and the shallow per-skill bodies fail this library's retirement standard since a base model with `<tool> --help` reconstructs most of them. The bundle as shipped is architecturally hostile to David's library — 42 always-on descriptions, a PAW MODE persona written into ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, broad `Bash(<tool> *)` wildcards including email-send, and a trivially bypassed regex safety hook all violate the strict-tiered footprint and architecture-contract Decisions 4/5. Best defensible use: never run `npx pawmode`; cherry-pick 2-4 individual SKILL.md files (standouts: c-email, c-calendar, c-screen, c-messaging) as on-demand personal skills only for services David actually uses and only after installing the wrapped CLI, rewriting frontmatter to house style per skill-authoring. Dependency burden (~35 single-maintainer binaries, per-service OAuth/tokens) and single-surface reach keep it out of HIGHLY RECOMMEND; MIT license permits free reuse of the cheat-sheet content.

verdicts.name: career-ops

inv.name: career-ops (santifer/career-ops)
inv.what_it_is: A full job-search operating system, not a lightweight skill: you clone the repo, seed it with your CV (cv.md), profile (config/profile.yml, modes/_profile.md), and writing samples, then drive everything through a single /career-ops router skill with ~30 modes. It evaluates pasted JDs/URLs with a structured A-G rubric (10 weighted dimensions plus a "Block G" ghost-job/legitimacy screen with liveness, blacklist, and geo-mismatch gates), generates ATS-optimized CVs (HTML-to-PDF and LaTeX paths with a zero-LLM fact validator that blocks invented metrics), scans Greenhouse/Ashby/Lever/company portals via Playwright and ATS APIs, runs interview prep/practice/debrief and offer-clause walkthroughs, tracks applications and follow-up cadence in markdown files with integrity/dedup scripts, and analyzes rejection patterns. It is deliberately anti-spray-and-pray (recommends not applying below 4.0/5) and encodes strong anti-fabrication rules (never invent claims not in cv.md/article-digest.md; tool-of-trade conflation explicitly forbidden). The author used it to evaluate 740+ listings and land a Head of Applied AI role; it has real press coverage (Business Insider, WIRED Greece) and a large contributor community (issue numbers past #1800).
inv.skills_inside: Single skill: one 182-line router SKILL.md (mirrored identically into .claude/, .agents/, .kimi/, .qwen/, .grok/, .opencode/, .antigravitycli/ for multi-CLI support) that dispatches on an argument to ~30 mode files in modes/ (5,475 lines total). Key modes, one line each: auto-pipeline (JD paste/URL -> evaluate + report + PDF + tracker); oferta (454-line A-G evaluation rubric with liveness/blacklist/geo gates and bounded research budget); ofertas (compare/rank multiple offers); scan (384 lines, portal discovery via Playwright/ATS APIs); pipeline (drain queued URLs from data/pipeline.md); batch (parallel subagent workers); pdf / latex / latex-tex (ATS CV generation, in-place .tex tailoring); cover / email (cover letters and draft-only application emails); apply (live form-filling assistant, generates answers from your story bank); contacto (find the right human + draft LinkedIn outreach); deep (company research prompt); interview-prep, interview/plan, interview/practice, interview/debrief (company-specific prep docs, time-blocked plans, mock Q&A, post-round gap closing); interview (profile/CV onboarding interview); offer-prep (319 lines, clause-by-clause offer/contract walkthrough + lawyer questions, explicitly not legal advice); patterns (rejection-pattern analysis); followup (cadence tracker with overdue flags and drafts); tracker / agent-inbox (status overview, cross-session queue); titles, upskill, training, project, add, update (adjacent-title suggestions, aggregate skill-gap, course/project evaluation, CV entry addition, self-update with diff preview); regional/eu-swe market calibration; plus _shared.md (339-line scoring system + source-of-truth rules), _profile/_custom templates, and translated mode sets in 15+ languages.
inv.quality: High — among the most substantive candidates in this domain. The mode files are dense procedure, not padded prose: exact gates with stop conditions, verbatim-quote requirements, hard WebSearch caps, explicit non-goals, and a source-of-truth boundary that forbids fabricated claims. It is backed by ~37K lines of Node tooling (84 root .mjs scripts incl. an 8K-line test suite, golden evals, dedup/merge/reconcile integrity checks, repost detection, funnel-velocity benchmarks, deterministic HTML CV renderer) plus a Go TUI dashboard. Maintenance is exceptional: v1.19.0 released the day of evaluation (2026-07-13), release-please, CodeQL, SBOM, dependency-review, and a no-user-data CI workflow; no telemetry found in scripts. It genuinely encodes things a base model does not do by default: the calibrated A-G rubric, ghost-job legitimacy signals (incl. AI-buzzword-vs-infrastructure mismatch), liveness gating before evaluation, compensation-reliability tiers, and hard anti-hallucination fences for CV content. Caveats: it is a whole application you live inside (your CV, tracker, and reports become repo working data — a big adoption commitment, not a drop-in skill); the router SKILL.md carries multi-CLI (Codex/Kimi/Qwen) plumbing prose irrelevant to a Claude-only user; and its CV/brand pipeline would overlap and need reconciling with the owner's existing brand-standard resume tooling (career-ops has its own templates/fonts/voice-dna system).
inv.dependencies: Node.js + npm (deps: playwright 1.61.1 with Chromium downloaded on postinstall, js-yaml, dotenv, @google/generative-ai); network access to job boards/ATS endpoints (Greenhouse, Ashby, Lever, Rippling, arbeitnow, etc.) for scan/liveness; Playwright browser tools (MCP browser_navigate/browser_snapshot or its own browser-extract.mjs) for URL evaluation, portal scans, and apply mode; WebSearch during evaluations (capped at 5 queries). Optional: Go (TUI dashboard), LaTeX/Overleaf for .tex export, API keys for headless no-agent paths (GEMINI_API_KEY, OPENROUTER_API_KEY, OpenAI-compatible endpoints, Ollama), and opt-in plugins requiring Apify/Notion/Gmail OAuth credentials. The core Claude Code flow needs no API keys — just the repo, Node, and Playwright.
inv.surfaces: Claude Code only (also runs on other agent CLIs — OpenCode, Codex, Gemini CLI, Qwen, Kimi — but not claude.ai). It requires a local repo working directory, Node script execution, Playwright browser automation, subagent delegation for scan/apply/batch, and persistent local data files (tracker, pipeline, reports); none of that survives a claude.ai skill upload, and the SKILL.md is a router into repo-relative files that would be missing.
inv.license: MIT (LICENSE file, Copyright 2026 Santiago Fernández de Valderrama; also declared in package.json, CITATION.cff, and SKILL.md frontmatter; separate TRADEMARK.md policy for the name/branding).

verdicts.overlaps_with: brand-standard (CV/resume/cover-letter/email voice, typography, and templates — career-ops ships its own voice-dna.md, fonts/, and cv-template system that would have to be overridden to defer to David's Eurostile/Poppins/Space Blue standard and his two voice registers); Claude Code deep-research built-in (career-ops's `deep` company-research mode); base model (one-off JD sanity checks and cover-letter drafting). The core machinery — A-G evaluation rubric with liveness/blacklist/geo gates, ghost-job legitimacy screening, Playwright ATS portal scanning, application tracker with dedup/repost detection, interview prep/practice/debrief cycle, offer clause walkthrough, zero-LLM CV fact validator — is genuinely net-new.
verdicts.surfaces: Claude Code only. The SKILL.md is a router into repo-relative mode files, Node scripts, and persistent data files (cv.md, tracker, pipeline, reports), and needs Playwright browser automation plus subagent delegation — none of which survives a claude.ai .skill upload. It cannot be installed into ~/.claude/skills either; it only works as its own cloned working repo.
verdicts.license: MIT (LICENSE file, Copyright 2026 Santiago Fernández de Valderrama; separate TRADEMARK.md covers only the name/branding)
verdicts.rationale: This is an exceptionally high-quality external application, not a library skill: verified dense procedure (454-line oferta rubric, weighted ghost-job signal table, compensation-reliability tiers, hard anti-fabrication fences) backed by ~37K lines of tested Node tooling, released the day of evaluation. What it adds is real and beyond base-model default — calibrated anti-spray-and-pray evaluation, liveness gating before effort is spent, ATS portal scanning, tracker/followup/rejection-pattern discipline. But its value is strictly episodic: it only earns its heavy adoption cost (clone repo, seed cv.md/profile/writing samples, live inside it as working data) during an active job search, and David appears to have just concluded one (started at Cutting Edge Communications, June 2026). It is Claude Code-only, failing half of his equal-weight surfaces, and its CV/voice pipeline must be reconciled to defer to brand-standard rather than its own voice-dna/templates. Disposition: do not add anything to the core library; bookmark the repo and clone it as its own project-scoped workspace if/when a job search begins — at that moment its effective priority jumps to adopt-immediately, with standout modes being oferta/auto-pipeline (evaluation + ghost screen), followup, interview/debrief, and offer-prep.

verdicts.name: agnix

inv.name: agnix
inv.what_it_is: agnix is not primarily a skill — it is a substantial Rust-workspace linter (~141K lines of Rust across 6 crates: agnix-core, agnix-rules, agnix-cli, agnix-lsp, agnix-mcp, agnix-wasm) that mechanically validates agent configuration files: SKILL.md, CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md, hooks in .claude/settings.json, *.mcp.json, plus Cursor/Copilot/Codex/Kiro/Gemini/Cline/OpenCode configs. The shortlist note said 156 rules; the repo has since grown to 432 rules (knowledge-base/rules.json, v1.1.0, last_updated 2026-07-10), 127 of them auto-fixable, each carrying evidence metadata (vendor-doc source URLs, verified_on date, MUST/SHOULD normative level, test-coverage flags). It ships a CLI with --fix/--fix-safe/--fix-unsafe, --strict, --watch, JSON/SARIF/GitHub-annotation output, plus an LSP server, an MCP server, a WASM browser playground, editor extensions (VS Code/JetBrains/Neovim/Zed), a GitHub Action, and a pre-commit hook. The Claude-facing surface is a thin SKILL.md wrapper plus a Claude Code plugin (skill + /agnix command + subagent) that shell out to the agnix binary. Note the repo README and package metadata now point at the agent-sh/agnix org (avifenesh/agnix redirects there).
inv.skills_inside: Not a skill collection; one skill shipped in two near-duplicate copies plus plugin scaffolding. (1) skills/agnix/SKILL.md — standalone skill: check agnix --version, install via cargo if missing, run agnix ., optionally agnix --fix ., re-validate; includes CLI reference and common-issue table. (2) plugin/skills/agnix/SKILL.md — same skill packaged for the Claude Code plugin, adds $ARGUMENTS parsing (--fix/--strict/--target) and rule-category/exit-code tables. (3) plugin/commands/agnix.md — /agnix slash command that spawns the subagent and formats results. (4) plugin/agents/agnix-agent.md — sonnet subagent that invokes the skill and returns structured JSON between AGNIX_RESULT markers. Plugin manifest at plugin/.claude-plugin/plugin.json (v0.37.5).
inv.quality: High-substance, exceptionally maintained. Evidence: last commit 2026-07-11 (2 days before evaluation date); CHANGELOG actively triages upstream tool releases (baselines pinned to Claude Code v2.1.206, Codex rust-v0.144.1, etc.) with dedicated spec-drift and tool-release-watch CI workflows, plus fuzzing, security, and codecov workflows; large fixture-based test suite (tests/fixtures with valid/invalid corpora per tool); a 3,500-line VALIDATION-RULES.md knowledge base with per-rule provenance. It genuinely encodes what a base model does not reliably know: exact, dated, vendor-verified frontmatter schemas and hook/MCP config constraints across 10+ fast-moving tools, checked deterministically rather than by judgment — a true mechanical supplement to the owner's diagnostics-and-tooling skill. Caveat: the SKILL.md wrappers themselves are thin (correctly so — they just drive the CLI), so all value depends on the binary being installed; also some auto-fixes are flagged unsafe/LOW-confidence and gated behind --fix-unsafe.
inv.dependencies: Requires the agnix CLI native binary — install via npm install -g agnix, brew install agent-sh/agnix/agnix, or cargo install agnix-cli (network needed once for install; linting itself runs fully offline, rules are compiled in). The skill's allowed-tools are Bash(agnix:*), Bash(cargo:*), Read, Glob, Grep. Optional components: agnix-lsp (LSP), agnix-mcp (MCP server binary), WASM playground, editor plugins, GitHub Action (agent-sh/agnix@v0). No API keys, no external services at lint time.
inv.surfaces: Claude Code only. The skill is a wrapper around a native Rust binary executed via Bash, and the plugin uses Claude Code plugin format (.claude-plugin/plugin.json, subagents, slash command); claude.ai cannot install or run the agnix CLI in its sandbox. The underlying validation is separately reachable outside Claude entirely (CLI, CI action, editors, browser playground), but as a Claude artifact it is Claude Code-specific.
inv.license: MIT OR Apache-2.0 (dual-licensed; LICENSE-MIT and LICENSE-APACHE at repo root; plugin.json declares MIT)

verdicts.overlaps_with: diagnostics-and-tooling (partial: SKILL.md frontmatter/name-matches-directory/trigger-language checks are the same ground as lint_skill.sh); minor adjacency to skill-authoring's shipping checklist and the built-in code-review/verify are NOT overlaps — nothing else in core or platform mechanically validates hooks, MCP, plugin, subagent, or memory-file schemas
verdicts.surfaces: Claude Code only (skill shells out to a native Rust binary via Bash; claude.ai's sandbox cannot install or run the agnix CLI). The underlying linter is also usable outside Claude entirely (CLI, GitHub Action, LSP, editors), which is a bonus but not a Claude surface.
verdicts.license: MIT OR Apache-2.0 (dual-licensed; LICENSE-MIT and LICENSE-APACHE at repo root; plugin.json declares MIT)
verdicts.rationale: Verified: agnix ships 432 evidence-backed rules (rules.json v1.1.0, updated 2026-07-10, per-rule vendor URLs + verified_on dates) covering not just SKILL.md but hooks in .claude/settings.json, *.mcp.json, plugins, subagents, and CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md across 10+ tools — the owner's diagnostics-and-tooling script covers ~7 SKILL.md-only checks, so agnix adds a large, genuinely net-new validation surface (hook/MCP/plugin schema correctness against current vendor specs) that neither the base model nor any built-in provides deterministically. It is exceptionally maintained (spec-drift CI pinned to dated tool baselines), which is exactly the fast-moving knowledge a model can't be trusted to recall. It stops short of MUST ADD because it is Claude Code-only, depends on installing an external native binary, its Claude-facing skill is a thin wrapper, and it cannot replace diagnostics-and-tooling (agnix knows nothing of the house Provenance/When-NOT sections, so both run side by side). Placement: project-scoped in claude-core-skills (and any repo carrying agent configs), where every session touches lintable artifacts; it does not earn always-on personal context cost since it's dead weight in non-agent-config sessions. Standout components: the standalone skills/agnix/SKILL.md wrapper is all David needs — the plugin/subagent/slash-command scaffolding and the near-duplicate plugin/skills copy are optional and can be skipped. One caution for adoption: use --fix-safe, not --fix-unsafe, and treat agnix warnings as advisory where they conflict with house style (house lint wins per architecture-contract).

verify.reasoning: Facts verified, tier refuted. VERIFIED (candidate files): rules.json at dives/12-agnix/crates/agnix-rules/ = 432 rules, v1.1.0, last_updated 2026-07-10, all 432 with evidence.source_urls + verified_on; spec-drift.yml/tool-release-watch.yml CI present; skills/agnix/SKILL.md is a thin CLI wrapper requiring `cargo install agnix-cli`. REDUNDANCY: net-new claim is true in rule count (CC-SK-001..021 validate model/context/allowed-tools/disable-model-invocation fields lint_skill.sh ignores; 27 hook + 26 MCP + 15 plugin + 17 subagent rules have no core or built-in counterpart) but hollow at the proposed placement — claude-core-skills contains NO .claude/settings.json, no *.mcp.json, no plugins, no subagents (verified by ls/find); the only in-scope artifacts are 14 SKILL.md files already owned by diagnostics-and-tooling's lint_skill.sh (which wins on conflict per architecture-contract Decision 2 precedence and the analyst's own 'house lint wins' caution) plus CLAUDE.md-class files where agnix's rules (CC-MEM-005 Generic Instruction, CC-MEM-007 Weak Constraint Language) are be-careful heuristics a strong base model applies unaided — the same standard that retired live-state-truth. ARCHITECTURE: no hard invariant break at project scope, but the wrapper's triggers ('validate skills', 'lint agent configs') collide with diagnostics-and-tooling's quoted triggers ('lint this skill', 'check every skill') — Decision 3 treats trigger collision as trust erosion — and a second advisory linter contradicts diagnostics-and-tooling's 'the script is the source of truth'; native-binary dependency is Claude Code-local-only. SUBSTANCE: passes (deterministic binary, dated vendor evidence, real CI), so NOT NEEDED is wrong; but zero measured behavioral delta on David's actual artifacts, and the unique validation surface (hooks/MCP/plugins/subagents) targets artifacts that are still an 'unexplored candidate' path per architecture-contract weak-point 3 (only one user-scope hook shipped 2026-07-12, outside any repo). Torn between HIGHLY RECOMMEND and NICE TO HAVE → mandated tie-break to the lower tier. Revisit upward if/when the library actually ships settings.json hooks, MCP, or plugin configs.

verdicts.name: pm-skills (product-on-purpose)

inv.name: pm-skills (product-on-purpose)
inv.what_it_is: A large, very actively maintained product-management skill library and Claude Code plugin covering the full Triple Diamond lifecycle (discover / define / develop / deliver / measure / iterate) plus foundation, sprint-tool, and utility families. The shortlist said 24 skills; the repo is now at v2.31.0 with 68 skills, 6 sub-agents (pm-critic, pm-skill-auditor, pm-changelog-curator, pm-release-conductor, pm-workflow-orchestrator, pm-skill-router), 11 workflow slash commands plus a /chain command, opt-in Node hooks (PreToolUse em-dash guardrail, SessionStart Triple Diamond phase router), and 227 sample outputs in library/. Every skill follows agentskills.io spec frontmatter and a fixed skeleton (When to Use / When NOT to Use with cross-references to sibling skills / numbered Instructions / Output Format bound to a references/TEMPLATE.md / Quality Checklist / references/EXAMPLE.md), producing PM artifacts such as PRDs, OKRs, interview syntheses, lean canvases, meeting recaps, and day-by-day Design Sprint and Foundation Sprint facilitation outputs. Last commit 2026-07-06 (one week before evaluation date); the repo self-polices with roughly 40 Node CI validators, per-skill HISTORY.md changelogs, and trigger-eval fixtures on 43 of 68 skills.
inv.skills_inside: Collection of 68 skills. DEFINE (5): define-hypothesis (testable hypothesis with success metrics); define-jtbd-canvas (Jobs-to-be-Done canvas); define-opportunity-tree (opportunity solution tree from outcome to solutions); define-prioritization-framework (runs RICE/ICE/MoSCoW/Weighted/Kano in parallel with cross-framework comparison); define-problem-statement (problem framing doc). DELIVER (6): deliver-acceptance-criteria (Given/When/Then criteria); deliver-edge-cases (whole-feature edge-case catalog); deliver-launch-checklist (cross-functional go/no-go checklist); deliver-prd (full PRD); deliver-release-notes (user-facing release notes); deliver-user-stories (story breakdown). DEVELOP (4): develop-adr (Nygard-format ADR); develop-design-rationale (design decision reasoning); develop-solution-brief (one-page solution pitch); develop-spike-summary (spike results with proceed/no recommendation). DISCOVER (5): discover-competitive-analysis (3-5 competitor comparison with 2x2 map); discover-interview-synthesis (cross-interview insight synthesis); discover-journey-map (journey stages/touchpoints/emotional curve); discover-market-sizing (TAM/SAM/SOM multi-framework triangulation with confidence grading); discover-stakeholder-summary (stakeholder mapping). FOUNDATION (11): foundation-build-risk-review (pre-build riskiest-assumption verdict); foundation-lean-canvas (nine-block lean canvas with optional HTML/SVG render); foundation-meeting-agenda (10 meeting-type agendas); foundation-meeting-brief (private pre-meeting strategy prep); foundation-meeting-recap (topic-segmented post-meeting summary); foundation-meeting-synthesize (cross-meeting pattern archaeology); foundation-okr-writer (5-mode OKR drafting/coaching with anti-fabrication refusals); foundation-persona (evidence-calibrated persona); foundation-prioritized-action-plan (Cynefin + Theory-of-Constraints action plan with downstream skill prompts); foundation-stakeholder-briefings (one master doc plus audience-tailored projections); foundation-stakeholder-update (async stakeholder comms with channel/audience variants). ITERATE (4): iterate-lessons-log (durable lessons entry); iterate-pivot-decision (pivot/persevere decision doc); iterate-refinement-notes (backlog refinement outcomes); iterate-retrospective (retro facilitation and doc). MEASURE (6): measure-dashboard-requirements (dashboard spec); measure-experiment-design (A/B test design); measure-experiment-results (experiment results with stats); measure-instrumentation-spec (analytics event tracking contract); measure-okr-grader (cycle-close OKR scoring with refusal rules); measure-survey-analysis (survey analysis with what-NOT-to-conclude warnings). TOOL — sprint facilitation (15): tool-design-sprint-readiness / -brief / -map-and-target (Mon) / -sketch (Tue) / -decide-and-storyboard (Wed) / -prototype-plan (Thu) / -test-and-score (Fri) — a complete Knapp-style Design Sprint week; tool-foundation-sprint-readiness / -brief / -basics / -differentiation / -approach-options / -magic-lenses / -founding-hypothesis — a complete 2-day Foundation Sprint; tool-note-and-vote (silent-ideation group decision mechanic). UTILITY (12): utility-mermaid-diagrams (valid mermaid across 15 diagram types); utility-pm-changelog-curator, utility-pm-critic (P0-P3 adversarial artifact review), utility-pm-release-conductor, utility-pm-skill-auditor (dispatch skills for the sub-agents); utility-pm-skill-builder / -iterate / -validate (skill authoring lifecycle); utility-pm-workflow-builder / -orchestrator (multi-skill chain authoring and governed execution); utility-slideshow-creator (JSON-spec presentations, 18 slide types); utility-update-pm-skills (self-update from GitHub releases).
inv.quality: High — the most engineering-disciplined skill library I have evaluated. Substantive, not padded: every skill has a consistent skeleton with numbered procedures, a fillable references/TEMPLATE.md, a completed references/EXAMPLE.md, a per-skill HISTORY.md, and reciprocal When-NOT-to-Use cross-links that disambiguate adjacent skills (e.g. acceptance-criteria vs edge-cases vs user-stories). 43/68 skills ship trigger-eval fixtures with train/holdout splits and CI routing-drift gates; ~40 Node validator scripts (each with its own test file) enforce count consistency, frontmatter validity, link integrity, and heading canon. Changelogs are unusually candid (v2.30.0 is explicitly a 'trust-repair release' fixing self-audited count drift). Recency is excellent: v2.31.0 tagged, last commit 2026-07-06. On base-model value-add: the underlying PM content (PRDs, OKRs, RICE, retros) is largely what a strong model already knows, but the library adds real value via fixed output contracts, anti-fabrication refusal rules (refuses to invent scores, baselines, statistical significance, emotional data), quality checklists, and the day-by-day Foundation/Design Sprint facilitation mechanics, which encode specific methodology a model would not reliably reproduce in that exact structure. Main weakness: 68 verbose descriptions loaded at once create context weight and routing-collision risk (partially mitigated by their pm-skill-router agent and eval fixtures), and some utility skills (release-conductor, changelog-curator, skill-auditor) are repo-internal maintenance tooling of no value to an end-user PM.
inv.dependencies: Core skills are pure markdown plus templates — no scripts, binaries, APIs, or network required at runtime (only non-markdown asset is one HTML template in foundation-lean-canvas). Optional extras: the two plugin hooks (guardrails, phase-router) require Node.js (repo pins via .nvmrc); sub-agents use standard Claude Code tools (Read/Grep/Glob); discover-market-sizing optionally uses WebFetch/WebSearch if available; utility-update-pm-skills and utility-pm-changelog-curator need network/git access to GitHub. The large scripts/ suite is repo CI/maintenance only, not needed by users. A companion MCP server exists (pm-skills-mcp) but is in maintenance mode and not required.
inv.surfaces: Both, with the full experience on Claude Code. Individual skills are self-contained SKILL.md + references markdown, so they package cleanly for claude.ai. The plugin layer — 6 sub-agents in agents/, 12 slash commands, hooks.json, .claude-plugin marketplace install — is Claude Code-specific. The repo also explicitly targets other agents: a native .codex-plugin manifest for Codex CLI, and dispatch skills claimed to work on Cursor, Windsurf, Copilot, and Gemini CLI.

verdicts.rationale: This is the highest-quality third-party library evaluated: 68 skills, all verified to follow a disciplined skeleton (When-NOT cross-links, numbered procedure, TEMPLATE.md-bound output, quality checklist, per-skill HISTORY.md), with 43/68 carrying trigger-eval fixtures and active maintenance (v2.31.0, last commit 2026-07-06). The PM lifecycle core is a whole-domain gain David's library lacks entirely, and it passes the live-state-truth retirement standard: fixed output contracts, anti-fabrication refusal rules (okr-grader, survey-analysis refuse to invent scores/significance), and especially the 15 day-by-day Design Sprint and Foundation Sprint facilitation skills (spot-checked magic-lenses: 105-min timebox, decider/backup-bet mechanics, mandatory custom lens) encode methodology a base model will not reproduce in that structure; standout sub-skills are the sprint families, deliver-prd, foundation-okr-writer, define-prioritization-framework, and the foundation-meeting-* family. It is not MUST ADD because PM is not among David's named daily domains and roughly 10 of 68 skills are redundant with his core (pm-critic vs adversarial-verify, the skill-builder trio vs his authoring stack, lessons-log vs retired lessons-ledger) or with platform built-ins (slideshow vs native pptx). Placement must be on-demand: 68 verbose always-on descriptions would blow the strict-tiered footprint and create routing-collision risk with his governors; install as a Claude Code plugin invoked deliberately (or project-scoped in a product repo) and upload a curated handful (prd, okr-writer, meeting family) as .skill files to claude.ai when product work actually arises, excluding the repo-maintenance utilities.

verify.reasoning:
CHECK 1 — REDUNDANCY (partially survives, weaker than claimed). The analyst's overlap map is factually accurate: I confirmed the 68-skill listing; utility-pm-critic/skill-builder/-iterate/-validate/lessons-log/slideshow/mermaid and the release/changelog/auditor utilities are redundant with adversarial-verify, the skill-authoring stack, retired lessons-ledger, the native pptx skill, base-model Mermaid, and repo-internal maintenance respectively — and the analyst already excluded them. The lifecycle core overlaps none of the 14 core skills or platform built-ins. BUT applying the library's own retirement standard (architecture-contract Decision 7: live-state-truth RETIRE-CONFIRMED because base models already do it, 8/8 cells zero delta) cuts deeper than the analyst allowed: deliver-prd/SKILL.md (read in full) is 8 numbered steps — problem, metrics, solution, requirements, scope, technical, risks, timeline — that Fable 5/Opus/Sonnet produce unprompted when asked for a PRD; its net-new value is template consistency, not behavior change. The same is plausibly true of most discover-*/define-*/deliver-*/iterate-* skills. Genuinely non-reproducible substance is concentrated in a minority: measure-okr-grader (5-value type enum, per-type scoring conventions, MUST NOT refusal rules against retro-shrinking committed scope, treating 0.7 as success on committed KRs, individual-performance use — verified in the file) and the 15 sprint facilitation skills (magic-lenses verified: 105-min timebox, decider-supervotes-last mechanics, mandatory custom lens, backup-must-differ-in-strategic-direction rule, Knapp/Character Capital sourcing). So the net-new claim is true for roughly a third of the library, thin for the rest — measurement by this library's own doctrine would likely retire much of the lifecycle core.

CHECK 2 — ARCHITECTURE (passes at the proposed placement). architecture-contract Decision 5 (activation footprint: every installed description costs every session) and invariant 5 (no ceremony on trivia) would be violated by 68 always-on descriptions, but the record prescribes on-demand placement (plugin/project-scoped, curated .skill uploads only when product work arises), which respects the footprint law. No invariant broken as placed.

CHECK 3 — SUBSTANCE (passes for the standouts, not uniformly). okr-grader and the sprint family carry fixed contracts, enum vocabularies, anti-fabrication refusals, and facilitation mechanics a strong model will not self-impose — real substance, not be-careful prose. The generic template skills are competent but largely reproduce baseline model behavior plus a TEMPLATE.md.

WHY THE TIER FALLS: HIGHLY RECOMMEND fails on demand evidence, which the analyst themselves conceded ('PM is not among David's named daily domains') but under-weighted. I found zero PM signal anywhere in David's environment: the README defines the library as governance + brand-standard; instructions/claude-ai-custom-instructions.md names his four installed skills and his external-artifact domain (resumes, cover letters, emails, proposals — career/job-search work, consistent with the separate career-ops dive); a repo-wide grep for PM/PRD/OKR hits only eval transcripts. Worse, the analyst's headline substance — the 15 sprint facilitation skills — requires a team, a Decider, and a facilitator in a multi-day workshop; a solo user with no evidenced product team cannot exercise exactly the skills that justify the tier. A high-quality library for a domain with no evidenced current or planned use, whose most defensible skills presuppose a context the user doesn't have, and whose generic majority would likely flunk this library's own measured-value bar, is the definition of NICE TO HAVE: worth bookmarking and installing on-demand if product work ever materializes, not a recommendation to act on now. Per the tie-breaking rule (torn between HIGHLY RECOMMEND and NICE TO HAVE, choose lower), the corrected tier is NICE TO HAVE.
